steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.content: To sketch the acceleration graph, we first need to find the function for acceleration, $a(t)$. Recall that acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with respect to time, which means it is the derivative of $v(t)$.

steps.title: Differentiate velocity
steps.content:
We differentiate the given velocity function $v(t) = 2t^3 - 9t^2 + 12t$ to find the acceleration. We apply the power rule from the data booklet ($f(x)=x^n \Rightarrow f'(x)=nx^{n-1}$) to each term:

$$\begin{aligned} a(t) &= v'(t) \\ &= \frac{d}{dt}(2t^3 - 9t^2 + 12t) \\ &= 2(3t^2) - 9(2t) + 12(1) \\ &= 6t^2 - 18t + 12 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the y-intercept
steps.content:
To find the y-intercept, we substitute $t=0$ into our acceleration function. This tells us where the graph starts on the vertical axis.

$$a(0) = 6(0)^2 - 18(0) + 12 = 12$$

So, the graph starts at the point $(0, 12)$.

steps.title: Find the x-intercepts
steps.content:
To find the x-intercepts (also called roots or zeros), we set the acceleration equal to zero and solve for $t$:

$$6t^2 - 18t + 12 = 0$$

We can simplify this by dividing the entire equation by 6:

$$t^2 - 3t + 2 = 0$$

Now, we factor the quadratic equation. We need two numbers that multiply to $+2$ and add to $-3$. These are $-1$ and $-2$:

$$(t - 1)(t - 2) = 0$$

This gives us two solutions:

$$t = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad t = 2$$

The domain is $t \geq 0$, so your sketch should start at the y-axis and extend to the right.

parts.content: Given that the particle is at the origin when $t=0$, find the position of the particle at the time when its velocity-time graph has a point of inflection.
parts.markscheme:
- Point of inflection of the velocity-time graph occurs when $a'(t)=0$: $12 t-18=0 \Rightarrow t=1.5$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Integrate $v(t)$ to find position: $s(t)=\int(2 t^{3}-9 t^{2}+12 t) dt = \frac{1}{2} t^{4}-3 t^{3}+6 t^{2}+C$
- Using initial condition $s(0)=0 \Rightarrow C=0$, so $s(t)=\frac{1}{2} t^{4}-3 t^{3}+6 t^{2}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Substitute $t=1.5$:
  $s(1.5)=\frac{1}{2}(1.5)^{4}-3(1.5)^{3}+6(1.5)^{2} \approx 5.91$ (or $\frac{189}{32}$) <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Strategy overview
steps.content:
To solve this problem, we need to find the position $s(t)$ at a specific instant in time. We will follow these steps:
1.  Find the time $t$ when the velocity-time graph has a **point of inflection**.
2.  Find the position function $s(t)$ by integrating the velocity function $v(t)$.
3.  Use the initial condition to find the constant of integration.
4.  Calculate the position at the time found in step 1.

steps.title: Find time of inflection
steps.content:
A point of inflection on the graph of $v(t)$ occurs when the second derivative of velocity is zero ($v''(t) = 0$).

First, we find the first derivative (acceleration) using the power rule $f'(x)=nx^{n-1}$:
$$v(t) = 2t^3 - 9t^2 + 12t$$
$$v'(t) = 6t^2 - 18t + 12$$

Now, we find the second derivative:
$$v''(t) = 12t - 18$$

Set this to zero to find the time:
$$12t - 18 = 0$$
$$12t = 18$$
$$t = 1.5 \text{ s}$$

steps.title: Integrate velocity for position
steps.content:
Position is the indefinite integral of velocity. We use the power rule for integration $\int x^n dx = \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1} + C$:

$$\begin{aligned} s(t) &= \int v(t) \, dt \\ &= \int (2t^3 - 9t^2 + 12t) \, dt \\ &= 2\left(\frac{t^4}{4}\right) - 9\left(\frac{t^3}{3}\right) + 12\left(\frac{t^2}{2}\right) + C \\ &= \frac{1}{2}t^4 - 3t^3 + 6t^2 + C \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find integration constant
steps.content:
We are given that the particle is at the origin when $t=0$, which means $s(0) = 0$. We substitute this into our position function:

$$0 = \frac{1}{2}(0)^4 - 3(0)^3 + 6(0)^2 + C$$
$$0 = 0 + C \implies C = 0$$

So, the specific position function is:
$$s(t) = \frac{1}{2}t^4 - 3t^3 + 6t^2$$

highlights.text: particle is at the origin when $t=0$

steps.title: Calculate final position
steps.content:
Finally, we substitute the time of the inflection point ($t = 1.5$) into the position function:

$$s(1.5) = \frac{1}{2}(1.5)^4 - 3(1.5)^3 + 6(1.5)^2$$

Using a calculator:
$$s(1.5) \approx 5.91 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Identify condition for inflection
steps.content:
To find the time when the velocity-time graph has a point of inflection, we need to find where the second derivative of velocity is equal to zero.

Mathematically, a point of inflection on the graph of $v(t)$ occurs when:
$$v''(t) = 0$$

steps.title: Find the second derivative
steps.content:
First, we differentiate the given velocity function $v(t)$ to find the acceleration $a(t) = v'(t)$, and then differentiate again to find $v''(t)$.

Using the power rule $f(x)=x^{n} \Rightarrow f'(x)=nx^{n-1}$:

$$\begin{aligned} v(t) &= 2t^3 - 9t^2 + 12t \\ v'(t) &= 3(2t^2) - 2(9t) + 1(12) \\ v'(t) &= 6t^2 - 18t + 12 \\ v''(t) &= 2(6t) - 18 \\ v''(t) &= 12t - 18 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for time
steps.content:
Now we set the second derivative to zero to find the time of the inflection point.

$$\begin{aligned} 12t - 18 &= 0 \\ 12t &= 18 \\ t &= \frac{18}{12} \\ t &= 1.5 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 18 divided by 12

ti84.instructions: Calculate 18 divided by 12

desmos.instructions: Calculate 18 divided by 12

steps.title: Setup the definite integral
steps.content:
Since the particle starts at the origin ($s(0)=0$), its position at time $t=1.5$ is equal to the displacement from $t=0$ to $t=1.5$. This can be calculated directly using the definite integral of velocity.

$$s(1.5) = \int_{0}^{1.5} v(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{1.5} (2t^3 - 9t^2 + 12t) \, dt$$

highlights.text: particle is at the origin when $t=0$

steps.title: Evaluate integral with calculator
steps.content:
We can evaluate this definite integral directly using a graphing calculator to find the final position.

$$\int_{0}^{1.5} (2t^3 - 9t^2 + 12t) \, dt \approx 5.91$$

gdc.instructions:
Go to Run-Matrix mode.
Press OPTN > CALC > ∫dx.
Enter the function: 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x
Set lower limit: 0, upper limit: 1.5
Press EXE

ti84.instructions:
Press MATH > 9 (fnInt).
Enter the function: 2X^3 - 9X^2 + 12X
Set limits from 0 to 1.5
Press ENTER

desmos.instructions:
Type 'int' to create an integral symbol.
Set bounds 0 and 1.5.
Type the function (2t^3 - 9t^2 + 12t)dt

calculator.description: Calculate the definite integral from 0 to 1.5

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The position of the particle at $t=1.5$ seconds is approximately $5.91$ meters.

$$s(1.5) = 5.91 \text{ m} \quad (\text{or } \frac{189}{32} \text{ m})$$

parts.content: Show that the first derivative of $f$ is $f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{x^{2}+2 x-3}{(x+1)^{2}}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Use quotient rule: Let $u=x^{2}-2 x+4, v=x+1$, so $u^{\prime}=2 x-2, v^{\prime}=1$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{(2 x-2)(x+1)-\left(x^{2}-2 x+4\right) \cdot 1}{(x+1)^{2}}=\frac{2 x^{2}-2-x^{2}+2 x-4}{(x+1)^{2}}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Simplify: $f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{x^{2}+2 x-3}{(x+1)^{2}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Find the $x$-coordinates of the local maximum and minimum points of $f$. Justify your answer using the first derivative test.
parts.markscheme:
- Set $f^{\prime}(x)=0: x^{2}+2 x-3=(x+3)(x-1)=0$, so $x=-3,1$ <marks>A1</marks>

- First derivative test: For $x<-3, f^{\prime}(x)>0$; for $-3<x<-1, f^{\prime}(x)<0$; for
$-1<x<1, f^{\prime}(x)<0$; for $x>1, f^{\prime}(x)>0$ <marks>R1</marks>
- Local maximum at $x=-3$, local minimum at $x=1$ <marks>A1 A1</marks>

parts.content: Determine the $x$-coordinate of the point of inflection of $f$. Justify your answer by analyzing the second derivative.
parts.markscheme:
- Find second derivative using quotient rule: Let $u=x^{2}+2 x-3, v=(x+1)^{2}$, so $u^{\prime}=2 x+2, v^{\prime}=2(x+1)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $f^{\prime \prime}(x)=\frac{(2 x+2)(x+1)^{2}-\left(x^{2}+2 x-3\right) \cdot 2(x+1)}{(x+1)^{4}}=\frac{2(x+1)\left[(x+1)^{2}-\left(x^{2}+2 x-3\right)\right]}{(x+1)^{4}}=\frac{8}{(x+1)^{3}}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Set $f^{\prime \prime}(x)=0$ : No real solutions, but check concavity change at $x=-1$ (undefined) <marks>M1</marks>
- No point of inflection since $f^{\prime \prime}(x)$ does not change sign <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Sketch the graph of $f$, indicating the local extrema, point of inflection, and vertical asymptote.
parts.markscheme:
- Sketch shows: Vertical asymptote at $x=-1$, local maximum at ( $-3,4$ ), local minimum at $(1,1)$, horizontal asymptote at $y=1$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Correct shape with intercepts and asymptotic behavior <marks>A1 A1</marks>

parts.content: Triangle ABC has $a = 8.1$ cm, $b = 12.3$ cm and area $15$ cm$^2$. Find the largest possible perimeter of triangle ABC.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute into the area formula: $15 = \frac{1}{2} \times 8.1 \times 12.3 \times \sin C$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate possible values for angle $C$: $\sin C = 0.301114$, $C = 17.5245^\circ$ or $C = 162.475^\circ$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify that the obtuse angle gives the largest perimeter: $C = 162.475^\circ$ or $\cos C < 0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Attempt to use the cosine rule: $c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute values into the cosine rule: $c^2 = (8.1)^2 + (12.3)^2 - 2(8.1)(12.3)\cos 162.475^\circ$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate the length of side $c$: $c = 20.1720$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate the final perimeter: $8.1 + 12.3 + 20.1720 = 40.5720$ 
  Perimeter $= 40.6$ cm <marks>A1</marks>

7 marks total

steps.title: Use area formula
steps.content:
To find the perimeter, we first need to find the angle $C$ between sides $a$ and $b$. We are given the area of the triangle, so we use the area formula from the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet.

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2}ab\sin C$$

Substituting the given values $a = 8.1$, $b = 12.3$, and $\text{Area} = 15$:

$$15 = \frac{1}{2}(8.1)(12.3)\sin C$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 0.5 * 8.1 * 12.3

ti84.instructions: Calculate 0.5 * 8.1 * 12.3

calculator.description: Calculate the coefficient of sin C

steps.title: Find angle C
steps.content:
First, we solve for $\sin C$:

$$\begin{aligned} 49.815 \sin C &= 15 \\ \sin C &= \frac{15}{49.815} \\ \sin C &\approx 0.301114 \end{aligned}$$

Now we find the angle $C$. Since sine is positive in both the first and second quadrants, there are two possible angles:

1. The acute angle: $C_1 = \arcsin(0.301114) \approx 17.5^\circ$
2. The obtuse angle: $C_2 = 180^\circ - 17.5^\circ \approx 162.5^\circ$

gdc.instructions:
1. Calculate arcsin(15/49.815)
2. Subtract the result from 180

ti84.instructions:
1. Calculate arcsin(15/49.815)
2. Subtract the result from 180

desmos.instructions: Type arcsin(15/49.815) and 180 - arcsin(15/49.815)

calculator.description: Calculate the possible angles for C

steps.title: Maximize perimeter
steps.content:
The question asks for the **largest possible perimeter**. The perimeter is $P = a + b + c$. Since $a$ and $b$ are fixed, we maximize the perimeter by maximizing side $c$.

In the cosine rule $c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$, the term $-2ab \cos C$ becomes larger when $\cos C$ is negative (obtuse angle). Therefore, the obtuse angle will produce the longest side $c$.

We select $C = 162.475^\circ$.

steps.title: Calculate side c
steps.content:
Using the cosine rule with the obtuse angle:

$$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab\cos C$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} c^2 &= 8.1^2 + 12.3^2 - 2(8.1)(12.3)\cos(162.475^\circ) \\ c &= \sqrt{8.1^2 + 12.3^2 - 2(8.1)(12.3)\cos(162.475^\circ)} \\ c &\approx 20.1720 \text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the full expression: sqrt(8.1^2 + 12.3^2 - 2*8.1*12.3*cos(162.475))

ti84.instructions: Enter the full expression: sqrt(8.1^2 + 12.3^2 - 2*8.1*12.3*cos(162.475))

steps.title: Calculate final perimeter
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the perimeter by summing all three sides:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Perimeter} &= 8.1 + 12.3 + 20.1720 \\ &= 40.5720 \text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, the largest possible perimeter is **40.6 cm**.

steps.title: Use area formula
steps.content:
To find the perimeter, we first need to determine the information about angle $C$. We start with the area formula from the data booklet.

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2}ab\sin C$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\begin{aligned} 15 &= \frac{1}{2}(8.1)(12.3)\sin C \\ 15 &= 49.815 \sin C \\ \sin C &= \frac{15}{49.815} \approx 0.301114 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the calculation:

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation:

desmos.instructions: Enter the calculation:

steps.title: Find cos C using identity
steps.content:
Instead of finding the angle $C$, we can find $\cos C$ directly using the Pythagorean identity. This is useful for the cosine rule later.

$$\cos^{2}C + \sin^{2}C = 1$$

Rearranging to solve for $\cos C$:

$$\cos C = \pm\sqrt{1 - \sin^2 C}$$

steps.title: Select the correct root
steps.content:
We need to choose between the positive or negative value for $\cos C$. The question asks for the **largest possible perimeter**, which implies we need the largest possible side $c$.

Looking at the cosine rule:

$$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$$

To maximize $c^2$, we need to subtract a negative number (making the term positive). Therefore, we select the negative value for $\cos C$ (which corresponds to an obtuse angle).

$$\begin{aligned} \cos C &= -\sqrt{1 - (0.301114)^2} \\ \cos C &= -\sqrt{1 - 0.09067} \\ \cos C &\approx -0.953588 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the negative square root:

ti84.instructions: Calculate the negative square root:

desmos.instructions: Calculate the negative square root:

steps.title: Apply cosine rule
steps.content:
Now substitute $\cos C$ into the cosine rule to find side $c$.

$$\begin{aligned} c^2 &= a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C \\ c^2 &= (8.1)^2 + (12.3)^2 - 2(8.1)(12.3)(-0.953588) \\ c^2 &= 65.61 + 151.29 - (199.26)(-0.953588) \\ c^2 &= 216.9 + 190.012 \\ c^2 &\approx 406.912 \end{aligned}$$

Taking the square root:

$$c \approx 20.1720$$

gdc.instructions: Compute c:

ti84.instructions: Compute c:

desmos.instructions: Compute c:

steps.title: Calculate final perimeter
steps.content:
Finally, sum the three side lengths to find the perimeter.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Perimeter} &= a + b + c \\ &= 8.1 + 12.3 + 20.1720 \\ &= 40.5720 \text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures:

$$\text{Perimeter} = 40.6 \text{ cm}$$

gdc.instructions: Add the sides:

ti84.instructions: Add the sides:

desmos.instructions: Add the sides:

steps.title: Analyze geometric setup
steps.content:
To find the perimeter, we need the length of the third side, $c$. We are given the area, side $a$, and side $b$. We can decompose the triangle into two right-angled triangles by dropping a height ($h$) from vertex B to the line extending from base $b$ (side AC).

Let's choose side $b$ as the base.

$$b = 12.3 \text{ cm}$$

steps.title: Calculate the height
steps.content:
Using the fundamental formula for the area of a triangle, $\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$, we can find the height $h$ corresponding to base $b$.

$$15 = \frac{1}{2} \times 12.3 \times h$$

Solving for $h$:

$$h = \frac{2 \times 15}{12.3} = \frac{30}{12.3} \approx 2.4390 \text{ cm}$$

steps.title: Find horizontal projection
steps.content:
Now consider the right-angled triangle formed by side $a$ (the hypotenuse), the height $h$, and the horizontal projection $p$ of side $a$. Using Pythagoras' theorem:

$$p^2 + h^2 = a^2$$

Substitute the values $a = 8.1$ and $h \approx 2.4390$:

$$\begin{aligned} p &= \sqrt{8.1^2 - (2.4390)^2} \\ p &\approx 7.7241 \text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Maximize side c
steps.content:
We need to find side $c$. The horizontal distance from vertex A to the perpendicular drop can be either $(b - p)$ or $(b + p)$, corresponding to an acute or obtuse angle at $C$.

To find the **largest possible perimeter**, we need side $c$ to be as long as possible. This occurs when the triangle is obtuse, extending the base length.

Total horizontal base length is $b + p$:

$$12.3 + 7.7241 = 20.0241 \text{ cm}$$

steps.title: Calculate side c
steps.content:
Now we calculate side $c$ using the large right-angled triangle with height $h$ and the total base length $(b+p)$.

$$\begin{aligned} c &= \sqrt{(b+p)^2 + h^2} \\ c &= \sqrt{(20.0241)^2 + (2.4390)^2} \\ c &\approx 20.172 \text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression directly:

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression directly:

desmos.instructions: Type the expression:

calculator.description: Calculate side c using the Pythagorean theorem

steps.title: Calculate final perimeter
steps.content:
Finally, add all side lengths to find the perimeter.

$$\begin{aligned} P &= a + b + c \\ &= 8.1 + 12.3 + 20.172 \\ &= 40.572 \text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures:

$$P = 40.6 \text{ cm}$$

parts.content: Show that the first derivative of $f(x)$ is $f'(x) = 3(x-1)^2$.
parts.markscheme:
- Differentiating $f(x) = x^3 - 3x^2 + 3x + m$:
          
	$f'(x) = 3x^2 - 6x + 3$ <marks>M1</marks> (for correct differentiation)
- Factorizing the derivative:
     
	$f'(x) = 3(x^2 - 2x + 1)$ <marks>A1</marks>
- $f'(x) = 3(x-1)^2$ <marks>A1</marks>(for correct factorization)

parts.content: Given that $f(0)=1$, determine the value of $m$. For this value of $m$, show that $f(x)$ has exactly one x-intercept.
parts.markscheme:
- Use the condition $f(0)=1$:
     
   $f(0) = (0)^3 - 3(0)^2 + 3(0) + m = 1$
       
	$m = 1$. <marks>A1</marks>
- For this value of $m$, the function is $f(x) = x^3 - 3x^2 + 3x + 1$.
   
	 From part 1, $f'(x) = 3(x-1)^2$.
   
	 Since $(x-1)^2 \ge 0$ for all real $x$, $f'(x) = 3(x-1)^2 \ge 0$ for all real $x$. <marks>R1</marks>
 - This means $f(x)$ is a strictly increasing function (it is only stationary at $x=1$). <marks>R1</marks>
- As $f(x)$ is continuous, and $\lim_{x \to -\infty} f(x) = -\infty$ and $\lim_{x \to \infty} f(x) = \infty$, by the Intermediate Value Theorem, $f(x)$ must cross the x-axis exactly once.<marks>A1 A1</marks>(one for limits, one for conclusion)

parts.content: Determine the x-coordinate of the point of inflection of $f$. Justify your answer by analyzing the second derivative.
parts.markscheme:
- Find the second derivative: $f''(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(3x^2 - 6x + 3) = 6x - 6$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Set $f''(x) = 0$:
  
	$6x - 6 = 0 \implies 6x = 6 \implies x = 1$. <marks>A1</marks>
 -  Check for sign change of $f''(x)$ around $x=1$:
    
	For $x < 1$, $f''(x) = 6x-6 < 0$ (concave down).
  
	For $x > 1$, $f''(x) = 6x-6 > 0$ (concave up). <marks>R1</marks>
- Since $f''(x)$ changes sign at $x=1$, there is a point of inflection at $x=1$. <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Find the exact coordinates of the stationary point and verify its nature using the second derivative test (or first derivative test if inconclusive).
parts.markscheme:
- From part 1, $f'(x) = 3(x-1)^2$. Setting $f'(x)=0 \implies x=1$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Find the y-coordinate of the stationary point using $m=1$:
 
  $f(1) = (1)^3 - 3(1)^2 + 3(1) + 1 = 1 - 3 + 3 + 1 = 2$.

   Coordinates of the stationary point: $(1, 2)$. <marks>A1</marks>
- Verify its nature using the second derivative test:
            $f''(x) = 6x - 6$.
            $f''(1) = 6(1) - 6 = 0$. <marks>R1</marks>
- Since $f''(1) = 0$, the second derivative test is inconclusive. We use the first derivative test. <marks>R1</marks>
-  From part 1, $f'(x) = 3(x-1)^2$.

   For $x < 1$, $(x-1)^2 > 0 \implies f'(x) > 0$.
   
	 For $x > 1$, $(x-1)^2 > 0 \implies f'(x) > 0$.
   
	 Since $f'(x)$ does not change sign around $x=1$ (it remains positive), the stationary point at $(1,2)$ is a stationary point of inflection (neither a local maximum nor a local minimum). <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Sketch the graph of $f$ for the value of $m$ found in part (b), indicating the x-intercept, stationary point, and point of inflection.
parts.markscheme:
- Function: $f(x) = x^3 - 3x^2 + 3x + 1$.
   
	 Stationary point (also point of inflection): $(1, 2)$.
   
	 y-intercept: $f(0)=1$. So $(0,1)$.
   
	 x-intercept: Set $f(x)=0 \implies x^3 - 3x^2 + 3x + 1 = 0$.
   
	 This can be rewritten as $(x^3 - 3x^2 + 3x - 1) + 2 = 0$.
   
	 $(x-1)^3 + 2 = 0 \implies (x-1)^3 = -2$.
   
	 $x-1 = \sqrt[3]{-2} = -\sqrt[3]{2}$.
    
	$x = 1 - \sqrt[3]{2}$. 
	
	This is the exact x-intercept. <marks>M1</marks>(for finding x-intercept or showing method)
            
	(Approximate value: $1 - \sqrt[3]{2} \approx 1 - 1.2599 \approx -0.26$)
 - The sketch should clearly show:
      
	- The general cubic shape for a monotonically increasing function with a stationary inflection point.
     -  Correctly plotted stationary point/point of inflection $(1,2)$.
    - Correctly plotted y-intercept $(0,1)$.
     -  The single x-intercept (approximately at $x \approx -0.26$).<marks>A1 A1</marks>
     
Note: <marks>A1</marks> for shape and stationary point, <marks>A1</marks> for intercepts

steps.title: Identify y-intercept condition
steps.content: The question asks for the coordinates of point A, which is where the graph intersects the y-axis. The **y-intercept** of any function always occurs when the x-coordinate is zero.

highlights.text: intersects the y-axis at point A

steps.title: Substitute x = 0
steps.content:

So, the y-coordinate is 2.

parts.content: Determine the derivative of the function $f(x)$.
parts.markscheme:
- The derivative of the function $f(x) = x^3 - 3x^2 + 2$ is found using the power rule:
  $$f'(x) = 3x^2 - 6x$$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content:
We are asked to find the derivative of the polynomial function $f(x) = x^3 - 3x^2 + 2$. We will differentiate each term individually using the Power Rule.

This involves concepts from **Topic 5: Calculus** (specifically SL 5.3—Differentiating polynomials).

highlights.text: Determine the derivative of the function $f(x)$

steps.title: State the Power Rule
steps.content:
From the **Calculus** section of the data booklet, we use the standard power rule for differentiation:

$$f(x)=x^{n} \Rightarrow f'(x)=nx^{n-1}$$

steps.title: Differentiate the first term
steps.content:
Applying the rule to the first term, $x^3$ (where $n=3$):

$$\frac{d}{dx}(x^3) = 3x^{3-1} = 3x^2$$

steps.title: Differentiate the second term
steps.content:
For the second term, $-3x^2$, we keep the constant multiplier $-3$ and apply the power rule to $x^2$ (where $n=2$):

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d}{dx}(-3x^2) &= -3 \cdot (2x^{2-1}) \\ &= -3(2x) \\ &= -6x \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate -3 * 2

ti84.instructions: Calculate -3 * 2

desmos.instructions: Calculate -3 * 2

steps.title: Differentiate the constant term
steps.content:
The derivative of any constant term is zero. Therefore, for the term $2$:

$$\frac{d}{dx}(2) = 0$$

steps.title: State the final derivative
steps.content:
Combining the differentiated terms gives the final answer:

$$f'(x) = 3x^2 - 6x$$

parts.content: Find the equation of the tangent line L at point A.
parts.markscheme:
- Evaluate the derivative at $x = 0$ to find the gradient of the tangent:
  $$f'(0) = 3(0)^2 - 6(0) = 0$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- State the equation of the tangent line (horizontal line through $y=2$):
  $$y = 2$$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Find coordinates of point A
steps.content:
First, we need the coordinates of point A. The question states that the graph intersects the y-axis at point A, which means the x-coordinate is 0.

Substitute $x = 0$ into the function $f(x)$:
$$f(0) = (0)^3 - 3(0)^2 + 2 = 2$$

So, point A is at coordinates $(0, 2)$.

steps.title: Differentiate the function
steps.content:
To find the gradient (slope) of the tangent line, we must find the derivative $f'(x)$. We apply the power rule for differentiation found in the data booklet.

For $f(x) = x^3 - 3x^2 + 2$:
- The derivative of $x^3$ is $3x^2$
- The derivative of $-3x^2$ is $-6x$
- The derivative of the constant $2$ is $0$

$$f'(x) = 3x^2 - 6x$$

steps.title: Calculate the gradient
steps.content:
Now, evaluate the derivative at the x-coordinate of point A ($x = 0$) to find the gradient of the tangent line.

$$f'(0) = 3(0)^2 - 6(0) = 0$$

The gradient of the tangent line is $m = 0$.

gdc.instructions: Use Analysis > Derivative > Value or simply d/dx template evaluated at x=0.

ti84.instructions: Use Math > 8:nDeriv. Enter the function, variable (X), and value (0).

desmos.instructions: Define f(x) and type f'(0).

steps.title: Write the tangent equation
steps.content:
We have the point $(0, 2)$ and the gradient $m = 0$. Using the point-slope form $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$:

$$\begin{aligned} y - 2 &= 0(x - 0) \\ y - 2 &= 0 \\ y &= 2 \end{aligned}$$

Since the gradient is 0, the tangent is a horizontal line passing through $y=2$.

steps.title: Verify graphically (Optional)

1. Plot $Y_1 = x^3 - 3x^2 + 2$
2. Select the "Tangent" operation at $x=0$
3. Observe the line drawn is horizontal at $y=2$

parts.content: Find the coordinates of point B, where the function has a local minimum.
parts.markscheme:
- Find the derivative: $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 6x$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Set $f'(x) = 0$ and solve for $x$:
  $$3x^2 - 6x = 0 \implies 3x(x - 2) = 0 \implies x = 0 \text{ or } x = 2$$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Determine which point is a minimum using the second derivative $f''(x) = 6x - 6$:
  $f''(0) = -6$ (local maximum)
  $f''(2) = 6$ (local minimum)

- Calculate the y-coordinate at $x=2$:
  $$f(2) = 2^3 - 3(2)^2 + 2 = 8 - 12 + 2 = -2$$

- State the coordinates of point B: $(2, -2)$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Find the first derivative
steps.content:
To find the stationary points, we first find the derivative of $f(x) = x^3 - 3x^2 + 2$ using the power rule $f(x)=x^{n} \Rightarrow f'(x)=nx^{n-1}$:

$$f'(x) = 3x^2 - 6x$$

steps.title: Solve for stationary points
steps.content:
Set the derivative equal to zero to find the stationary points:

$$\begin{aligned} 3x^2 - 6x &= 0 \\ 3x(x - 2) &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

This gives two possible $x$-values:
$$x = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad x = 2$$

gdc.instructions: Go to Equation > Solver. Enter 3x^2 - 6x = 0 and solve.

ti84.instructions: Press [MATH] > [B] Solver. Enter eqn: 0 = 3X^2 - 6X. Solve for X. Note there are two roots.

desmos.instructions: Graph y = 3x^2 - 6x and find the x-intercepts.

calculator.description: Find the roots of the derivative

steps.title: Find the second derivative
steps.content:
To determine the nature of these stationary points (maximum or minimum), we find the second derivative $f''(x)$ by differentiating $f'(x)$:

$$f''(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(3x^2 - 6x) = 6x - 6$$

steps.title: Apply second derivative test
steps.content:
Substitute the $x$-values into $f''(x)$ to identify the local minimum. A positive second derivative ($f''(x) > 0$) indicates a local minimum.

For $x = 0$:
$$f''(0) = 6(0) - 6 = -6$$ 
Since $-6 < 0$, the graph is concave down, so $x=0$ is a **local maximum**.

For $x = 2$:
$$f''(2) = 6(2) - 6 = 6$$
Since $6 > 0$, the graph is concave up, so $x=2$ is a **local minimum**.

steps.title: Calculate the y-coordinate
steps.content:
Now we find the $y$-coordinate of the local minimum by substituting $x = 2$ back into the original function $f(x)$:

$$\begin{aligned} f(2) &= (2)^3 - 3(2)^2 + 2 \\ &= 8 - 12 + 2 \\ &= -2 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Type 2^3 - 3(2)^2 + 2 and press [EXE].

ti84.instructions: Type 2^3 - 3(2)^2 + 2 and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type f(2) if f(x) is defined, or calculate 2^3 - 3(2)^2 + 2 directly.

steps.title: State coordinates of B
steps.content:
The coordinates of point B, the local minimum, are:

$$(2, -2)$$

steps.title: Find the derivative
steps.content:
To find the stationary points (maxima and minima), we first need to find the gradient function $f'(x)$.

Using the power rule from the **Calculus** section of the data booklet ($$f(x)=x^{n} \Rightarrow f'(x)=nx^{n-1}$$):

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= x^3 - 3x^2 + 2 \\ f'(x) &= 3x^{3-1} - 3(2x^{2-1}) + 0 \\ f'(x) &= 3x^2 - 6x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for stationary points
steps.content:
Stationary points occur where the derivative is equal to zero.

Set $f'(x) = 0$ and solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} 3x^2 - 6x &= 0 \\ 3x(x - 2) &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

This gives two solutions:
*   $3x = 0 \implies x = 0$
*   $x - 2 = 0 \implies x = 2$

gdc.instructions: In Equation mode, solve Polynomial degree 2.

ti84.instructions: Press [MATH] > [B:Solver] or graph Y1 = 3x^2 - 6x and find Zeroes.

desmos.instructions: Type equation to see vertical lines at solutions.

calculator.description: Find the roots of the derivative

steps.title: Identify the local minimum
steps.content:
We need to determine which $x$-value corresponds to the local minimum (point B).

Recall the shape of a **positive cubic function** ($y = +x^3...$):
*   It starts from bottom-left and ends at top-right.
*   The curve goes **up** to a maximum, then **down** to a minimum, then **up** again.
*   Therefore, the first stationary point (smaller $x$) is the **maximum**.
*   The second stationary point (larger $x$) is the **minimum**.

Since $x=0$ is the smaller value, it corresponds to the local maximum (point A).
Thus, **$x=2$ corresponds to the local minimum (point B)**.

steps.title: Calculate the y-coordinate
steps.content:
Substitute $x = 2$ back into the original function $f(x)$ to find the $y$-coordinate of B.

$$\begin{aligned} f(2) &= (2)^3 - 3(2)^2 + 2 \\ &= 8 - 3(4) + 2 \\ &= 8 - 12 + 2 \\ &= -2 \end{aligned}$$

desmos.instructions: Define f(x) then evaluate f(2).

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The coordinates of point B are:

$$(2, -2)$$

steps.title: Identify point B
steps.content:
We need to determine which $x$-value corresponds to point B. 

*   The question states that point A is where the graph intersects the $y$-axis, which corresponds to $x=0$.
*   Therefore, the **local minimum at point B** must be at $x = 2$.

steps.title: Determine the normal slope
steps.content:
At the local minimum B ($x=2$), the derivative is zero ($f'(2)=0$), meaning the tangent line is horizontal (slope $m_t = 0$).

The normal line is perpendicular to the tangent. A line perpendicular to a horizontal line is a **vertical line**. The slope of a vertical line is undefined.

steps.title: State the equation
steps.content: A vertical line passing through $x = 2$ has the equation $x = 2$. This is the equation of the normal line at B.

steps.title: Strategy
steps.content:
To find the equation of the normal line at point B, we first need to determine the coordinates of B. Since the question states there is a **local minimum at point B**, we can find this point using a graphing calculator (GDC). 

Once we have the coordinates, we will analyze the slope of the curve at that minimum to determine the equation of the normal line.

steps.title: Analyze the minimum point
steps.content:
The calculator shows the local minimum B is at the point $(2, -2)$.

At any local minimum (or maximum), the tangent line is perfectly horizontal. A horizontal line has a slope of 0.

$$m_{\text{tangent}} = 0$$

steps.title: Determine normal slope
steps.content:
The normal line is perpendicular to the tangent line. Since the tangent is horizontal, the normal line must be **vertical**.

A vertical line has an undefined slope and is always written in the form $x = k$, where $k$ is the x-coordinate of the point.

steps.title: State the equation
steps.content:
Since the normal line passes through point B at $(2, -2)$ and is vertical, its equation is simply the x-coordinate:

$$x = 2$$

parts.content: Find the value of $k$ and the coordinates of the vertex of the graph of $y=f(x)$.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute the point $(1,2)$ into $f(x)$: $f(1)=-(1)^{2}+4(1)+k=-1+4+k=2$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Solve for $k$: $3+k=2 \Longrightarrow k=-1$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Find the vertex coordinate $x$: For $f(x)=-x^{2}+4 x-1$, vertex at $x=-\frac{b}{2 a}=-\frac{4}{2(-1)}=2$

- Evaluate $f(2)$: $f(2)=-(2)^{2}+4(2)-1=-4+8-1=3$ <marks>A1</marks>

- State the vertex: $(2,3)$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Show that the graph of $y=f(x)$ has no points of inflection.
parts.markscheme:
- Compute the second derivative:
$f(x)=-x^{2}+4 x-1$, $f^{\prime}(x)=-2 x+4$, $f^{\prime \prime}(x)=-2$ <marks>A1</marks>

- State that $f^{\prime \prime}(x)=-2 \neq 0$ for all $x$, so there are no points where $f^{\prime \prime}(x)=0$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Conclude that no points of inflection exist <marks>R1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Find the first derivative
steps.content:
To analyze the points of inflection, we first need to find the derivative of the function $f(x)=-x^{2}+4 x+k$. 

Using the power rule from the data booklet ($f(x)=x^{n} \Rightarrow f'(x)=nx^{n-1}$), where the derivative of a constant $k$ is $0$:

$$f'(x) = -2x + 4$$

steps.title: Find the second derivative
steps.content:
Next, we differentiate $f'(x)$ to find the second derivative, $f''(x)$. This derivative describes the concavity of the function.

$$f''(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(-2x + 4) = -2$$

steps.title: Check for points of inflection
steps.content:
A point of inflection occurs where the concavity changes, which requires $f''(x) = 0$ (or is undefined). 

We observe that our second derivative is a non-zero constant:

$$f''(x) = -2 \neq 0$$

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
Since $f''(x)$ is never equal to zero, the concavity of the graph never changes (it is always negative, meaning the graph is always concave down).

Therefore, the graph of $y=f(x)$ has **no points of inflection**.

parts.content: Sketch the graph of $y=f(x)$ for $-1 \leq x \leq 5$, clearly showing the vertex, the point $(1,2)$, and any axis intercepts.
parts.markscheme:
- Sketch correct parabolic shape, opening downward, over $-1 \leq x \leq 5$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Vertex marked at $(2, 3)$ and point $(1, 2)$ shown <marks>A1</marks>

- Axis intercepts indicated: $y$-intercept at $(0,-1)$; $x$-intercepts by solving $-x^{2}+4 x-1=0$, giving roots at $x=2 \pm \sqrt{3}$ (approximately $0.27$ and $3.73$) <marks>A1</marks>

questions.specification: A particle moves in a straight line with velocity given by $v(t)=6 t-t^{2}$, for $t ≥ 0$, where velocity is in metres per second and time is in seconds.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > SL 5.9—Kinematics problems > Question Type 3: Going from acceleration and velocity, to velocity and displacement
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Going from acceleration and velocity, to velocity and displacement
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: This is a kinematics question: given velocity v(t), part (a) asks for the time of maximum velocity (using dv/dt = acceleration and setting it to 0), and part (b) asks for displacement over an interval (integrating velocity).

parts.content: Find the time when the particle reaches its maximum velocity.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize that maximum velocity occurs when $\frac{dv}{dt}=0$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate derivative: $6-2t=0$
- $t=3$ seconds <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the optimization condition
steps.content:
To find the maximum velocity, we must find the stationary point of the velocity function. In calculus, a maximum occurs when the derivative of the function with respect to time is equal to zero.

We need to find where:
$$\frac{dv}{dt} = 0$$

steps.title: Differentiate the velocity function
steps.content:
We differentiate the given velocity function $v(t) = 6t - t^2$ with respect to $t$. We apply the power rule from the data booklet to each term.

Using the rule for $x^n$:
- For $6t$ (where $n=1$): the derivative is $6$
- For $-t^2$ (where $n=2$): the derivative is $-2t$

Therefore:
$$v'(t) = 6 - 2t$$

steps.title: Solve for time
steps.content:
Set the derivative equal to zero and solve for $t$:

$$\begin{aligned} 6 - 2t &= 0 \\ 6 &= 2t \\ t &= \frac{6}{2} \\ t &= 3 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Equation mode

The derivative is zero when $t = 3$. Therefore, the particle reaches its maximum velocity at 3 seconds.

steps.title: Identify quadratic form
steps.content:
The velocity function is given as a quadratic equation. To make it easier to work with, we rewrite it in the standard form $$v(t) = at^2 + bt + c$$:

$$v(t) = -t^2 + 6t$$

steps.title: Analyze the parabola
steps.content:
In this quadratic, the coefficient of $t^2$ is $a = -1$. Since $a < 0$, the graph is a downward-opening parabola. This means the maximum velocity occurs at the vertex (the highest point) of the parabola.

We identify the coefficients:
$$\begin{aligned} a &= -1 \\ b &= 6 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Use axis of symmetry formula
steps.content: To find the time $t$ at the vertex, we use the axis of symmetry formula found in the **Functions** section of the data booklet. Although the booklet uses $x$, we apply it to our variable $t$.

steps.title: Calculate the time
steps.content:
Substitute $a = -1$ and $b = 6$ into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} t &= -\frac{6}{2(-1)} \\ &= -\frac{6}{-2} \\ &= 3 \end{aligned}$$

parts.content:
Calculate the displacement of the particle from $t=0$ to $t=4$.
![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/ae9e55cb-ae75-4c47-933d-ce3b5fe7a0b2.png)
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to integrate velocity to find displacement <marks>M1</marks>
- $s=\int_{0}^{4} (6t-t^2) \, dt$
- Correct integration: $\left[3t^2 - \frac{1}{3}t^3\right]_0^4$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Substitute limits: $\left(3(16) - \frac{64}{3}\right) - 0$
- $s = \frac{80}{3} \approx 26.7 \text{ m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Set up the definite integral
steps.content:
To find the displacement of the particle, we need to integrate the velocity function $v(t)$ with respect to time over the interval from $t=0$ to $t=4$. Displacement is the definite integral of velocity:

$$s = \int_{0}^{4} v(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{4} (6t - t^2) \, dt$$

steps.title: Find the antiderivative
steps.content:
We find the antiderivative using the power rule for integration from the **Calculus** section of the data booklet. The rule states:

$$\int x^{n}dx=\frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$$

Applying this to our function term by term:
- For $6t$ (where $n=1$): $\int 6t^1 \, dt = 6 \cdot \frac{t^2}{2} = 3t^2$
- For $-t^2$ (where $n=2$): $\int -t^2 \, dt = -\frac{t^3}{3}$

steps.title: Apply Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
steps.content:
Now we evaluate the definite integral by substituting the upper limit ($4$) and the lower limit ($0$) into our antiderivative:

$$\left[3t^2 - \frac{1}{3}t^3\right]_0^4$$

Substituting the values:

$$s = \left(3(4)^2 - \frac{1}{3}(4)^3\right) - \left(3(0)^2 - \frac{1}{3}(0)^3\right)$$

steps.title: Calculate the final displacement
steps.content:
Simplify the expression to find the final numerical answer:

$$\begin{aligned} s &= \left(3(16) - \frac{64}{3}\right) - 0 \\ &= 48 - \frac{64}{3} \\ &= \frac{144}{3} - \frac{64}{3} \\ &= \frac{80}{3} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

As a decimal, this is approximately $26.7$ m.

gdc.instructions: In Run-Matrix mode, press OPTN > CALC > ∫dx. Enter ∫(6x-x^2, 0, 4).

steps.title: Recall displacement formula
steps.content:
To find the displacement of a particle over a specific time interval, we calculate the definite integral of the velocity function. This concept is covered in **Topic 5: Calculus** (specifically **SL 5.9 Kinematics** and **SL 5.11 Definite integrals**).

$$s = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} v(t) \, dt$$

steps.title: Set up the definite integral
steps.content:
We need to calculate the displacement from $t=0$ to $t=4$. We substitute the given velocity function $v(t) = 6t - t^2$ and the time limits into the integral expression.

$$\text{Displacement} = \int_{0}^{4} (6t - t^2) \, dt$$

Setting up this integral expression is sufficient to earn the method mark (**M1**).

The calculator returns the value $26.666\dots$, which is exactly $\frac{80}{3}$. We write the final answer to 3 significant figures.

$$s = 26.7 \text{ m}$$

questions.specification: A function $g$ is defined by $g(x)=x^{2} \ln |x|$, where $x \neq 0$. The graph of $g$ has a point of inflection at $x=e^{a}$.

parts.content: Find the value of $a$, and show that the second derivative changes sign at this point.
parts.markscheme:
Compute the second derivative of $g(x)=x^{2} \ln |x|$.

First derivative:
$g^{\prime}(x)=2 x \ln |x|+x^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{x}=2 x \ln |x|+x$
<marks>A1</marks>

Second derivative:
$g^{\prime \prime}(x)=2 \ln |x|+2 x \cdot \frac{1}{x}+1=2 \ln |x|+3$
<marks>A1</marks>

Point of inflection at $x=e^{a}$:
$g^{\prime 
\prime}\left(e^{a}\right)=2 \ln \left|e^{a}\right|+3=2 a+3=0 \Longrightarrow a=-\frac{3}{2}$
<marks>M1</marks> <marks>A1</marks>

Check sign change:
For $x=e^{-2}$, $g^{\prime \prime}\left(e^{-2}\right)=2(-2)+3=-1<0$;
for $x=e^{-1}$, $g^{\prime \prime}\left(e^{-1}\right)=2(-1)+3=1>0$.
Sign change confirms inflection point.
<marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Recall the product rule
steps.content:
To find the derivatives of $g(x) = x^2 \ln|x|$, we need to use the product rule. The product rule states that for a function $y = uv$, the derivative is:

$$y' = u v' + v u'$$

Here, we set $u = x^2$ and $v = \ln|x|$.

steps.title: Find the first derivative
steps.content:
We differentiate the components using the standard derivatives from the data booklet:
- For $u = x^2$, the derivative is $u' = 2x$ using $$f(x)=x^n \Rightarrow f'(x)=nx^{n-1}$$
- For $v = \ln|x|$, the derivative is $v' = \frac{1}{x}$ using $$f(x)=\ln x \Rightarrow f'(x)=\frac{1}{x}$$

Applying the product rule:

$$\begin{aligned} g'(x) &= x^2 \left(\frac{1}{x}\right) + \ln|x|(2x) \\ &= x + 2x \ln|x| \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the second derivative
steps.content:
To find the inflection point, we need the second derivative $g''(x)$. We differentiate $g'(x) = x + 2x \ln|x|$ again.

The derivative of the first term $x$ is $1$. For the second term $2x \ln|x|$, we use the product rule again with $u=2x$ and $v=\ln|x|$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d}{dx}(2x \ln|x|) &= 2x\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) + \ln|x|(2) \\ &= 2 + 2\ln|x| \end{aligned}$$

Combining these:

$$\begin{aligned} g''(x) &= 1 + (2 + 2\ln|x|) \\ &= 3 + 2\ln|x| \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for a
steps.content:
An inflection point occurs where the concavity changes, which corresponds to $g''(x) = 0$. We are given that the inflection point is at $x = e^a$.

Substitute $x = e^a$ into the second derivative equation. Note that $e^a$ is always positive, so $\ln|e^a| = \ln(e^a) = a$.

$$\begin{aligned} 3 + 2\ln(e^a) &= 0 \\ 3 + 2a &= 0 \\ 2a &= -3 \\ a &= -\frac{3}{2} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value:

ti84.instructions: Calculate the value:

desmos.instructions: Calculate the value:

steps.title: Test for sign change
steps.content:
To prove this is an inflection point, we must show $g''(x)$ changes sign at $x = e^{-1.5}$. We verify this analytically by testing values on either side.

Choose $x_1 = e^{-2}$ (since $-2 < -1.5$) and $x_2 = e^{-1}$ (since $-1 > -1.5$).

Test $x_1 = e^{-2}$:
$$g''(e^{-2}) = 3 + 2\ln(e^{-2}) = 3 + 2(-2) = 3 - 4 = -1$$
Since $-1 < 0$, the graph is concave down.

Test $x_2 = e^{-1}$:
$$g''(e^{-1}) = 3 + 2\ln(e^{-1}) = 3 + 2(-1) = 3 - 2 = 1$$
Since $1 > 0$, the graph is concave up.

gdc.instructions: Calculate 3 + 2*(-2) and 3 + 2*(-1)

ti84.instructions: Calculate 3 + 2*(-2) and 3 + 2*(-1)

desmos.instructions: Calculate 3 + 2*(-2) and 3 + 2*(-1)

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
The second derivative changes sign from negative to positive at $x = e^{-1.5}$. Therefore, there is a point of inflection at this value.

Answer: $$a = -\frac{3}{2}$$

steps.title: Find the first derivative
steps.content:
To find the points of inflection, we first need to find the derivatives of the function $g(x) = x^2 \ln|x|$. We apply the product rule with $u = x^2$ and $v = \ln|x|$.

Using the derivatives from the data booklet:
- $\frac{d}{dx}(x^n) = nx^{n-1}$ gives $u' = 2x$
- $\frac{d}{dx}(\ln x) = \frac{1}{x}$ gives $v' = \frac{1}{x}$

$$\begin{aligned} g'(x) &= (2x)(\ln|x|) + (x^2)\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) \\ g'(x) &= 2x \ln|x| + x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the second derivative
steps.content:
Now we differentiate $g'(x) = 2x \ln|x| + x$ to find the second derivative. We use the product rule again for the first term.

For $2x \ln|x|$:
- Derivative of $2x$ is $2$
- Derivative of $\ln|x|$ is $\frac{1}{x}$

So, $\frac{d}{dx}(2x \ln|x|) = 2\ln|x| + 2x(\frac{1}{x}) = 2\ln|x| + 2$.

The derivative of the last term $x$ is $1$.

Adding these together:
$$\begin{aligned} g''(x) &= (2\ln|x| + 2) + 1 \\ g''(x) &= 2\ln|x| + 3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for a
steps.content:
An inflection point occurs where the second derivative is zero. We are given that the inflection point is at $x = e^a$. Substitute this into our expression for $g''(x)$ and set it to zero.

$$2\ln|e^a| + 3 = 0$$

Since $e^a$ is always positive, $|e^a| = e^a$. Using the log law $\ln(e^k) = k$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2(a) + 3 &= 0 \\ 2a &= -3 \\ a &= -\frac{3}{2} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate -3 / 2

ti84.instructions: Calculate -3 / 2

desmos.instructions: Calculate -3 / 2

calculator.description: Verify the solution for the equation 2a + 3 = 0

steps.title: Graphical analysis of sign change
steps.content:
To confirm this is a point of inflection, we must show $g''(x)$ changes sign. We sketch the graph of the second derivative function $y = 2\ln|x| + 3$.

**Graph Features:**
- This is a logarithmic curve shifted up by 3.
- It has a vertical asymptote at $x=0$.
- For $x > 0$, the function is strictly increasing.

By graphing $y = 2\ln x + 3$ on your GDC, you can clearly see the curve starts below the x-axis (negative) and crosses to above the x-axis (positive) at the root $x = e^{-1.5}$.

This "crossing" of the x-axis visually demonstrates that $g''(x)$ changes sign at $x = e^a$, confirming it is an inflection point.

steps.content: The value is $a = -\frac{3}{2}$. The graph of the second derivative $y = 2\ln|x| + 3$ crosses the x-axis at this point, confirming the change in sign required for a point of inflection.

parts.content: Sketch the graph of $g^{\prime \prime}(x)$ for $x>0$, indicating the $x$-intercept and the behavior as $x \rightarrow 0^{+}$ and $x \rightarrow \infty$.
parts.markscheme:
For $x>0$, $g^{\prime 
\prime}(x)=2 \ln x+3$.
$x$-intercept: $2 \ln x+3=0 \Longrightarrow \ln x=-\frac{3}{2} \Longrightarrow x=e^{-3 / 2}$.
As $x \rightarrow 0^{+}$, $\ln x \rightarrow-\infty$, so $g^{\prime \prime}(x) \rightarrow-\infty$. As $x \rightarrow \infty$, $g^{\prime \prime}(x) \rightarrow \infty$.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/e67f06d3-ae94-40cf-8ea5-747795089d49.png)

Award **A1** for correct shape (concave down, increasing).
Award **A1** for $x$-intercept at $e^{-3/2}$.
Award **A1** for asymptotic behavior as $x \to 0$.
<marks>A1</marks>
<marks>A1</marks>
<marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Simplify the function
steps.content:
Since the question asks for the sketch for $x > 0$, we can simplify the absolute value term because $|x| = x$ when $x$ is positive.

$$g(x) = x^2 \ln x$$

steps.title: Find the first derivative
steps.content:
To find $g''(x)$, we first need $g'(x)$. We apply the **product rule** ($uv' + vu'$) using the formulas for $x^n$ and $\ln x$ from the data booklet.

Let $u = x^2$ and $v = \ln x$.
Then $u' = 2x$ and $v' = \frac{1}{x}$.

$$\begin{aligned} g'(x) &= x^2 \left(\frac{1}{x}\right) + (2x) \ln x \\ &= x + 2x \ln x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the second derivative
steps.content:
Now we differentiate $g'(x) = x + 2x \ln x$ to get $g''(x)$.

- The derivative of $x$ is $1$.
- For $2x \ln x$, we use the product rule again ($u=2x, v=\ln x$):
  $$\frac{d}{dx}(2x \ln x) = 2x\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) + 2 \ln x = 2 + 2 \ln x$$

Combining these:
$$\begin{aligned} g''(x) &= 1 + (2 + 2 \ln x) \\ &= 3 + 2 \ln x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the x-intercept
steps.content:
To find the $x$-intercept of the graph of $g''(x)$, we set the function equal to zero and solve for $x$. Using the definition of logarithms ($y = \ln x \iff x = e^y$):

$$\begin{aligned} 2 \ln x + 3 &= 0 \\ 2 \ln x &= -3 \\ \ln x &= -\frac{3}{2} \\ x &= e^{-3/2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine limits
steps.content:
We examine the behavior of $g''(x) = 2 \ln x + 3$ at the boundaries:

1.  **As $x \to 0^+$:**  
    We know that $\ln x \to -\infty$.  
    Therefore, $2 \ln x + 3 \to -\infty$.

2.  **As $x \to \infty$:**  
    We know that $\ln x \to \infty$.  
    Therefore, $2 \ln x + 3 \to \infty$.

steps.title: Sketch the graph
steps.content:
Combine the features to draw the sketch for $x > 0$:

*   **Shape:** An increasing logarithmic curve (concave down).
*   **x-intercept:** Mark the point labeled $e^{-3/2}$ on the positive x-axis.
*   **Behavior:** The curve starts from $-\infty$ near the y-axis (asymptotic to $x=0$) and rises continuously towards $+\infty$ as $x$ increases.

steps.title: Find the second derivative
steps.content:
To sketch $g''(x)$, we first need to find its equation by differentiating $g(x)$ twice. For $x>0$, we can treat $\ln|x|$ as $\ln x$.

First derivative using the product rule:
$$\begin{aligned} g(x) &= x^2 \ln x \\ g'(x) &= (2x)(\ln x) + (x^2)\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) \\ &= 2x \ln x + x \end{aligned}$$

Second derivative:
$$\begin{aligned} g''(x) &= \frac{d}{dx}(2x \ln x) + \frac{d}{dx}(x) \\ &= \left(2 \ln x + 2x \cdot \frac{1}{x}\right) + 1 \\ &= 2 \ln x + 2 + 1 \\ &= 2 \ln x + 3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify graph transformations
steps.content:
We can analyze $g''(x) = 2 \ln x + 3$ using **Graph Transformations** on the parent function $y = \ln x$.

1.  **Vertical Stretch:** The term $2 \ln x$ represents a vertical stretch by a scale factor of 2.
2.  **Vertical Translation:** The $+3$ represents a shift upwards by 3 units.

The parent function $y = \ln x$ is strictly increasing and has a vertical asymptote at $x=0$. These transformations preserve the general shape and the asymptote location.

steps.title: Calculate the x-intercept
steps.content:
The parent function $y = \ln x$ has an $x$-intercept at $x=1$. Since our graph is shifted up by 3, the new intercept will occur where the parent function had a negative value (to the left of $x=1$).

We set $g''(x) = 0$ to find the exact intercept:
$$\begin{aligned} 2 \ln x + 3 &= 0 \\ 2 \ln x &= -3 \\ \ln x &= -\frac{3}{2} \\ x &= e^{-3/2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine asymptotic behavior
steps.content:
We examine the behavior of $g''(x) = 2 \ln x + 3$ at the boundaries defined by the parent function behavior:

*   **As $x \to 0^+$:** We know $\ln x \to -\infty$. Applying the transformations ($2(-\infty) + 3$) results in $-\infty$. The graph goes down along the vertical asymptote $x=0$.
*   **As $x \to \infty$:** We know $\ln x \to \infty$. Applying the transformations ($2(\infty) + 3$) results in $\infty$. The graph increases without bound.

steps.title: Sketch the graph
steps.content:
Combine these features to sketch the graph:

1.  **Shape:** Draw a curve that is concave down and always increasing (like $\ln x$).
2.  **Intercept:** Mark the $x$-intercept at $x = e^{-3/2}$ (approx $0.22$).
3.  **Asymptote:** The $y$-axis ($x=0$) is the vertical asymptote.
4.  **Behavior:** The curve starts from $-\infty$ near the $y$-axis and rises indefinitely as $x$ increases.

This matches the markscheme requirements for shape, intercept, and limits.

steps.title: Find the first derivative
steps.content:
To graph the second derivative $g''(x)$, we first need to find its equation analytically. We start by finding the first derivative of $g(x) = x^2 \ln x$ using the product rule.

Let $u = x^2$ and $v = \ln x$.
Then $u' = 2x$ and $v' = \frac{1}{x}$.

Using the product rule $$(uv)' = u'v + uv'$$:
$$g'(x) = (2x)(\ln x) + (x^2)\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)$$
$$g'(x) = 2x \ln x + x$$

steps.title: Find the second derivative
steps.content:
Now we differentiate $g'(x) = 2x \ln x + x$ to find $g''(x)$.

For the term $2x \ln x$, we use the product rule again:
$$\frac{d}{dx}(2x \ln x) = (2)(\ln x) + (2x)\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) = 2 \ln x + 2$$

For the term $x$, the derivative is $1$. Adding these together:
$$\begin{aligned} g''(x) &= (2 \ln x + 2) + 1 \\ g''(x) &= 2 \ln x + 3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Visualize with GDC
steps.content:
Use your graphing calculator to plot $y = 2 \ln x + 3$. This visualization helps confirm the shape and asymptotic behavior required for the sketch.

From the graph, observe that:
1. The curve is increasing and concave down.
2. As $x$ gets close to 0, the graph plunges downwards (vertical asymptote).
3. The graph crosses the x-axis at a small positive value.

1. Go to Graph mode
2. Enter y = 2ln(x) + 3
3. Set View Window to x: 0 to 5, y: -5 to 5
4. Draw

2. Enter Y1 = 2*ln(X) + 3
3. Press [WINDOW] and set Xmin=0, Xmax=5, Ymin=-5, Ymax=5
4. Press [GRAPH]

steps.title: Find exact x-intercept
steps.content:
The question requires the exact $x$-intercept. We solve for $x$ where $g''(x) = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2 \ln x + 3 &= 0 \\ 2 \ln x &= -3 \\ \ln x &= -\frac{3}{2} \\ x &= e^{-3/2} \end{aligned}$$

Exponentiating both sides results in $x = e^{-3/2}$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate e^(-1.5)

ti84.instructions: Calculate e^(-1.5) to check position on graph

desmos.instructions: Type e^(-1.5)

calculator.description: Verify the decimal value of the intercept

steps.title: Determine asymptotic behavior
steps.content:
Analyze the limits as requested:

**As $x \rightarrow 0^+$:**
We know that $\ln x \rightarrow -\infty$. Therefore:
$$g''(x) = 2 \ln x + 3 \rightarrow -\infty$$
This indicates a vertical asymptote on the negative y-axis.

**As $x \rightarrow \infty$:**
We know that $\ln x \rightarrow \infty$. Therefore:
$$g''(x) \rightarrow \infty$$
The graph continues to increase without bound.

steps.title: Draw the sketch
steps.content:
Combine these features to draw the final sketch:

1.  **Shape**: Draw a curve in the first and fourth quadrants that is increasing and concave down (like a log curve).
2.  **Intercept**: Label the point where it crosses the x-axis as $e^{-3/2}$.
3.  **Asymptote**: Ensure the line goes down towards $-\infty$ as it approaches the y-axis ($x=0$).
4.  **End Behavior**: Show the line extending upwards to the right as $x$ increases.

parts.content: Find the area enclosed by the graph of $g^{\prime \prime}(x)$ and the $x$-axis from $x=e^{a}$ to $x=e^{a+1}$, assuming $g^{\prime \prime}(x)$ is positive in this interval.
parts.markscheme:
Area: $\int_{e^{-3 / 2}}^{e^{-1 / 2}}(2 \ln x+3) d x$
<marks>M1</marks>

Antiderivative:
$\int(2 \ln x+3) d x=2(x \ln x -x)+3 x = 2x \ln x + x$
<marks>M1</marks>

Evaluate:
$[2 x \ln x + x]_{e^{-3 / 2}}^{e^{-1 / 2}}$
Upper limit ($x=e^{-1/2}$):
$2 e^{-1 / 2} \cdot\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)+e^{-1 / 2} = -e^{-1/2} + e^{-1/2} = 0$
<marks>A1</marks>

Lower limit ($x=e^{-3/2}$):
$2 e^{-3 / 2} \cdot\left(-\frac{3}{2}\right)+e^{-3 / 2} = -3 e^{-3 / 2}+e^{-3 / 2} = -2 e^{-3 / 2}$
<marks>A1</marks>

Area $= 0 - (-2e^{-3/2}) = 2 e^{-3 / 2}$
<marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Find the first derivative
steps.content:
To apply the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus later, we first need the expression for $g'(x)$. We use the product rule on $g(x)=x^{2} \ln x$.

Let $u = x^2$ and $v = \ln x$. Then $u' = 2x$ and $v' = \frac{1}{x}$.

$$\begin{aligned} g'(x) &= u'v + uv' \\ &= 2x(\ln x) + x^2\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) \\ &= 2x \ln x + x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the inflection point
steps.content:
An inflection point occurs when $g''(x) = 0$ (and changes sign). First, we find $g''(x)$ by differentiating $g'(x) = 2x \ln x + x$.

Using the product rule on $2x \ln x$:
$$\frac{d}{dx}(2x \ln x) = 2 \ln x + 2x\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) = 2 \ln x + 2$$

So,
$$g''(x) = (2 \ln x + 2) + 1 = 2 \ln x + 3$$

Now set $g''(x) = 0$ to find the inflection point:
$$\begin{aligned} 2 \ln x + 3 &= 0 \\ 2 \ln x &= -3 \\ \ln x &= -\frac{3}{2} \\ x &= e^{-3/2} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate e^(-1.5)

ti84.instructions: Calculate e^(-1.5) to verify the value

desmos.instructions: Type e^(-1.5)

steps.title: Identify integration limits
steps.content:
The problem states the inflection point is at $x = e^a$. From our calculation, $x = e^{-3/2}$, so $a = -\frac{3}{2}$.

The integration interval is from $e^a$ to $e^{a+1}$:
- Lower limit: $e^a = e^{-3/2}$
- Upper limit: $e^{a+1} = e^{-3/2 + 1} = e^{-1/2}$

steps.title: Apply Fundamental Theorem
steps.content:
The area is given by the integral of $g''(x)$ from the lower to the upper limit. By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, the antiderivative of the second derivative $g''(x)$ is simply the first derivative $g'(x)$.

$$\text{Area} = \int_{e^{-3/2}}^{e^{-1/2}} g''(x) \, dx = \left[ g'(x) \right]_{e^{-3/2}}^{e^{-1/2}}$$ 

We use the expression for $g'(x)$ we found in Step 0:
$$g'(x) = 2x \ln x + x$$

highlights.text: Find the area enclosed by the graph of $g^{\prime \prime}(x)$ and the $x$-axis

gdc.instructions: Calculate 2*exp(-0.5)*ln(exp(-0.5)) + exp(-0.5)

calculator.description: Verify the evaluation at the upper limit

gdc.instructions: Calculate 2*exp(-1.5)*ln(exp(-1.5)) + exp(-1.5)

calculator.description: Verify the evaluation at the lower limit

steps.title: Calculate final area
steps.content:
Subtract the lower limit value from the upper limit value to find the exact area:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Area} &= g'(e^{-1/2}) - g'(e^{-3/2}) \\ &= 0 - (-2e^{-3/2}) \\ &= 2e^{-3/2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the limits of integration
steps.content:
First, we need to find the value of $a$ to determine our limits of integration. We are told that $g(x)$ has a **point of inflection** at $x = e^a$. A point of inflection occurs where the second derivative equals zero.

Set $g''(x) = 0$:
$$2 \ln x + 3 = 0$$

Solving for $x$:
$$\begin{aligned} 2 \ln x &= -3 \\ \ln x &= -\frac{3}{2} \\ x &= e^{-3/2} \end{aligned}$$

Comparing this to $x = e^a$, we see that $a = -\frac{3}{2}$.

Our limits of integration are:
- Lower limit: $x = e^a = e^{-3/2}$
- Upper limit: $x = e^{a+1} = e^{-3/2 + 1} = e^{-1/2}$

gdc.instructions: Equation Solver > Solver > Eq: 2lnx + 3 = 0

ti84.instructions: Graph Y1 = 2ln(X) + 3 and find the zero.

desmos.instructions: Type equation to find intersection with x-axis

calculator.description: Verify the solution for x where the second derivative is zero

steps.title: Set up the definite integral
steps.content:
The question asks for the area enclosed by the graph of $g''(x)$ and the $x$-axis. We integrate $g''(x)$ between our limits.

$$ \text{Area} = \int_{e^{-3/2}}^{e^{-1/2}} (2 \ln x + 3) \, dx $$

highlights.text: Area enclosed by the graph of $g^{\prime \prime}(x)$

steps.title: Apply Integration by Parts to ln(x)
steps.content:
To integrate the term $2 \ln x$, we use the method of **integration by parts**. The formula is $\int u \frac{dv}{dx} dx = uv - \int v \frac{du}{dx} dx$.

For $\int \ln x \, dx$:
- Let $u = \ln x \Rightarrow \frac{du}{dx} = \frac{1}{x}$
- Let $\frac{dv}{dx} = 1 \Rightarrow v = x$

Applying the formula:
$$\begin{aligned} \int \ln x \, dx &= x \ln x - \int x \cdot \frac{1}{x} \, dx \\ &= x \ln x - \int 1 \, dx \\ &= x \ln x - x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the full antiderivative
steps.content:
Now we combine the results to find the antiderivative of the entire function $2 \ln x + 3$.

$$\begin{aligned} \int (2 \ln x + 3) \, dx &= 2 \int \ln x \, dx + \int 3 \, dx \\ &= 2(x \ln x - x) + 3x \\ &= 2x \ln x - 2x + 3x \\ &= 2x \ln x + x \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Run-Mat > Optn > Calc > ∫dx. Enter ∫(2lnx+3, e^(-1.5), e^(-0.5))

ti84.instructions: Math > 9:fnInt. Enter fnInt(2ln(X)+3, X, e^(-1.5), e^(-0.5))

desmos.instructions: Type int for integral symbol, then enter limits and function

calculator.description: Verify the definite integral calculation numerically

steps.title: State the final area
steps.content:
Finally, subtract the lower limit value from the upper limit value to find the area.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Area} &= \text{Upper} - \text{Lower} \\ &= 0 - (-2e^{-3/2}) \\ &= 2e^{-3/2} \end{aligned}$$

The area is $2e^{-3/2}$.

steps.title: Find the second derivative
steps.content:
First, we need to find $g''(x)$ to determine the function we are integrating.

Given $g(x) = x^2 \ln x$, we apply the product rule to find the first derivative:
$$g'(x) = (2x)(\ln x) + (x^2)\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) = 2x \ln x + x$$

Now, differentiate again to find the second derivative:
$$\begin{aligned} g''(x) &= \left(2 \ln x + 2x \cdot \frac{1}{x}\right) + 1 \\ &= (2 \ln x + 2) + 1 \\ &= 2 \ln x + 3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the limits of integration
steps.content:
The problem states there is a point of inflection at $x=e^a$. A point of inflection occurs where $g''(x) = 0$.

Set $g''(x) = 0$ and solve for $x$:
$$\begin{aligned} 2 \ln x + 3 &= 0 \\ \ln x &= -\frac{3}{2} \\ x &= e^{-3/2} \end{aligned}$$

Matching this to $x=e^a$, we find that $a = -\frac{3}{2}$.

The interval for the area is from $x=e^a$ to $x=e^{a+1}$.
Lower limit: $e^{-3/2}$
Upper limit: $e^{-3/2 + 1} = e^{-1/2}$

We need to calculate the Area:
$$\text{Area} = \int_{e^{-3/2}}^{e^{-1/2}} (2 \ln x + 3) \, dx$$

steps.title: Apply integration by substitution
steps.content:
As per the solution method, we use the substitution $u = \ln x$.

Rearrange to express $x$ and $dx$ in terms of $u$:
$$u = \ln x \Rightarrow x = e^u$$
$$dx = e^u \, du$$

Now, change the limits of integration from $x$-values to $u$-values:
- Lower limit ($x = e^{-3/2}$): $u = \ln(e^{-3/2}) = -\frac{3}{2}$
- Upper limit ($x = e^{-1/2}$): $u = \ln(e^{-1/2}) = -\frac{1}{2}$

Substitute these into the integral:
$$\int_{-3/2}^{-1/2} (2u + 3)e^u \, du$$

steps.title: Solve using integration by parts
steps.content:
We now solve $\int (2u+3)e^u \, du$ using integration by parts formula $\int w \, dv = wv - \int v \, dw$.

Let $w = 2u+3$, so $dw = 2 \, du$.
Let $dv = e^u \, du$, so $v = e^u$.

Applying the formula:
$$\begin{aligned} \int (2u+3)e^u \, du &= (2u+3)e^u - \int e^u(2) \, du \\ &= (2u+3)e^u - 2e^u \\ &= e^u(2u + 3 - 2) \\ &= e^u(2u + 1) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Evaluate the definite integral
steps.content:
Finally, we evaluate the result at the limits $u = -\frac{1}{2}$ and $u = -\frac{3}{2}$:

$$[e^u(2u + 1)]_{-3/2}^{-1/2}$$

Substitute the upper limit ($u = -\frac{1}{2}$):
$$e^{-1/2}\left(2\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) + 1\right) = e^{-1/2}(-1 + 1) = 0$$

Substitute the lower limit ($u = -\frac{3}{2}$):
$$e^{-3/2}\left(2\left(-\frac{3}{2}\right) + 1\right) = e^{-3/2}(-3 + 1) = -2e^{-3/2}$$

Calculate the area (Upper $-$ Lower):
$$\text{Area} = 0 - (-2e^{-3/2}) = 2e^{-3/2}$$

parts.content: Find the first derivative $f'(x)$ of the function $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 1$.
parts.markscheme:
- Find the first derivative: $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the method
steps.content: To find the derivative $f'(x)$, we need to differentiate each term of the polynomial $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 1$ separately using the power rule.

steps.title: Recall the Power Rule
steps.content:
According to the **Calculus** section of the data booklet, the derivative of a power function is given by:

$$f(x)=x^{n} \Rightarrow f'(x)=nx^{n-1}$$

steps.title: Differentiate the first term
steps.content:
For the first term $x^3$, apply the rule with $n=3$:

$$\frac{d}{dx}(x^3) = 3x^{3-1} = 3x^2$$

steps.title: Differentiate the remaining terms
steps.content:
For the other terms, multiply the coefficient by the power and reduce the power by 1:

*   **$-6x^2$**: Multiply $-6$ by $2$ to get $-12$, then reduce the power to $x^1$. Result: $-12x$.
*   **$9x$**: The derivative of $x$ is $1$. Result: $9(1) = 9$.
*   **$1$**: The derivative of any constant is $0$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate -6 * 2

ti84.instructions: Calculate -6 * 2

desmos.instructions: Type -6 * 2

calculator.description: Calculate coefficient for the second term

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Combining all the differentiated terms gives the final derivative:

$$f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9$$

parts.content: Determine the critical points of the function by solving $f'(x) = 0$.
parts.markscheme:
- Set the first derivative equal to zero: $3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Divide by 3:
  $x^2 - 4x + 3 = 0$

- Factorize the quadratic equation:
  $(x - 3)(x - 1) = 0$

- State the critical points: $x = 3$ and $x = 1$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Recall the condition for critical points
steps.content:
To find the critical points (also called stationary points) of a function, we need to find where its gradient is zero. This implies finding the derivative $f'(x)$ and setting it equal to zero.

This relates to **Topic 5.8** regarding testing for maximum and minimum points.

steps.title: Differentiate the function
steps.content:
We find the derivative of $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 1$ using the power rule from the data booklet:

$$f(x)=x^{n} \Rightarrow f'(x)=nx^{n-1}$$

Applying this to each term:
- The derivative of $x^3$ is $3x^2$
- The derivative of $-6x^2$ is $-12x$
- The derivative of $9x$ is $9$
- The derivative of the constant $1$ is $0$

So, the derivative is:
$$f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9$$

steps.title: Set derivative to zero
steps.content:
To find the critical points, we set the derivative equal to zero:

$$3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 0$$

steps.title: Simplify the equation
steps.content:
Notice that all terms are divisible by 3. We can simplify the quadratic equation by dividing the entire equation by 3:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{3x^2}{3} - \frac{12x}{3} + \frac{9}{3} &= \frac{0}{3} \\ x^2 - 4x + 3 &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Factorize the quadratic
steps.content:
Now we factorize the simplified quadratic $x^2 - 4x + 3 = 0$. We are looking for two numbers that:
- Multiply to give $+3$
- Add to give $-4$

The numbers are $-3$ and $-1$. Therefore, we can write the equation in factored form:

$$(x - 3)(x - 1) = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
Finally, we find the values of $x$ that make the equation true by setting each factor to zero:

$$\begin{aligned} x - 3 &= 0 \implies x = 3 \\ x - 1 &= 0 \implies x = 1 \end{aligned}$$

The critical points are at $x = 1$ and $x = 3$.

steps.title: Verify with calculator
steps.content: Since you have access to a graphing calculator, it is good practice to verify these roots. You can graph the derivative function or use a polynomial solver.

1. Go to Equation mode.
2. Select Polynomial (F2).
3. Set Degree to 2.
4. Enter coefficients: a=3, b=-12, c=9.
5. Press Solve (F1).

2. Select 'Root Finder'.
3. Set Order to 2.
4. Enter coefficients: a=3, b=-12, c=9.
5. Press [SOLVE].

calculator.description: Find the roots of the derivative 3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 0

steps.title: Differentiate the function
steps.content:
First, we find the derivative of $f(x)$ to determine the gradient function. We apply the power rule for each term:

$$f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 1$$

Using the power rule formula:
$$f'(x) = 3x^{3-1} - 6(2x^{2-1}) + 9(1) + 0$$
$$f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9$$

steps.title: Set derivative to zero
steps.content:
To find the critical points, we set the derivative equal to zero. This step is awarded the method mark **M1**.

$$3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 0$$

To make the coefficients smaller for the quadratic formula, we divide the entire equation by 3:

$$x^2 - 4x + 3 = 0$$

highlights.text: Set the first derivative equal to zero

steps.title: Apply the quadratic formula
steps.content:
Now we identify the coefficients from our simplified quadratic equation $x^2 - 4x + 3 = 0$:

$$a = 1, \quad b = -4, \quad c = 3$$

We substitute these values into the quadratic formula to solve for $x$:

$$x = \dfrac{-(-4) \pm \sqrt{(-4)^2 - 4(1)(3)}}{2(1)}$$

$$x = \dfrac{4 \pm \sqrt{16 - 12}}{2}$$

steps.title: Calculate the roots
steps.content:
Simplify the expression inside the square root (the discriminant) and solve for both values of $x$:

$$x = \dfrac{4 \pm \sqrt{4}}{2}$$
$$x = \dfrac{4 \pm 2}{2}$$

This gives us two solutions:

1. $x = \frac{4 + 2}{2} = \frac{6}{2} = 3$
2. $x = \frac{4 - 2}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1$

gdc.instructions: Equation > Polynomial > Degree 2 > Enter a=1, b=-4, c=3 > Solve

ti84.instructions: Apps > PolySmlt2 > Poly Root Finder > Order: 2 > Enter coeffs: a=1, b=-4, c=3 > Solve

desmos.instructions: Type the equation to see the x-intercepts

calculator.description: Verify the roots of the quadratic equation

steps.title: State the critical points
steps.content:
The critical points are the values of $x$ we found.

**Answer:**
$$x = 1 \text{ and } x = 3$$

This matches the final **A1** mark in the markscheme.

parts.content: Find the second derivative $f''(x)$ of the function $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 1$.
parts.markscheme:
- Find the second derivative: $f''(x) = 6x - 12$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Understand the Method
steps.content:
To find the second derivative $f''(x)$, we use the method of **Successive Differentiation**. This means we must differentiate the original function $f(x)$ twice:

1.  Find the first derivative, $f'(x)$.
2.  Differentiate $f'(x)$ to get the second derivative, $f''(x)$.

This concept is covered in **SL 5.7—The second derivative**.

steps.title: Find the First Derivative
steps.content:
First, we apply the power rule to each term of $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 1$.

The power rule states:

$$f(x)=x^{n} \Rightarrow f'(x)=nx^{n-1}$$

Applying this to each term:
- For $x^3$: multiply by $3$ and reduce power by $1$ $\rightarrow 3x^2$
- For $-6x^2$: multiply $-6$ by $2$ and reduce power by $1$ $\rightarrow -12x$
- For $9x$: this is $9x^1$, so we get $9(1)x^0$ $\rightarrow 9$
- For $1$: the derivative of a constant is $0$

So, the first derivative is:
$$f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9$$

steps.title: Find the Second Derivative
steps.content:
Now, we differentiate $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9$ to find $f''(x)$. Applying the power rule again:

- For $3x^2$: multiply $3$ by $2$ and reduce power by $1$ $\rightarrow 6x$
- For $-12x$: this is $-12x^1$, so we get $-12(1)x^0$ $\rightarrow -12$
- For $9$: the derivative of a constant is $0$

Combining these gives:
$$f''(x) = 6x - 12$$

steps.title: State Final Answer
steps.content:
The second derivative is:

$$f''(x) = 6x - 12$$

This matches the markscheme requirement for **A1**.

parts.content: Determine whether the critical points are local maxima or local minima.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the second derivative: $f''(x) = 6x - 12$

- Substitute $x = 3$ into $f''(x)$:
  $f''(3) = 6(3) - 12 = 18 - 12 = 6$ (positive)
  Conclude local minimum at $x = 3$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute $x = 1$ into $f''(x)$:
  $f''(1) = 6(1) - 12 = 6 - 12 = -6$ (negative)
  Conclude local maximum at $x = 1$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the critical points
steps.content:
To determine the nature of the critical points, we first need to know where they are. We find them by setting the first derivative to zero.

$$f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 1$$

$$f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9$$

Setting $f'(x) = 0$:

$$3(x^2 - 4x + 3) = 0$$
$$3(x-1)(x-3) = 0$$

So, our critical points are at **$x = 1$** and **$x = 3$**.

steps.title: Find the second derivative
steps.content:
Using the Second Derivative Test requires us to find $f''(x)$. We differentiate $f'(x)$ using the power rule from the data booklet.

$$f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9$$

$$f''(x) = 6x - 12$$

steps.title: Test the first critical point
steps.content:
We test the point $x = 1$ by substituting it into the second derivative.

$$f''(1) = 6(1) - 12 = -6$$

Since $f''(1) < 0$ (negative), the function is **concave down** at this point. This indicates a **local maximum**.

steps.title: Test the second critical point
steps.content:
Now we test the point $x = 3$ by substituting it into the second derivative.

$$f''(3) = 6(3) - 12 = 6$$

Since $f''(3) > 0$ (positive), the function is **concave up** at this point. This indicates a **local minimum**.

steps.title: Verify with GDC
steps.content: You can visually verify these results by graphing the function on your calculator. You should see a "peak" (maximum) at $x=1$ and a "valley" (minimum) at $x=3$.

3. Draw the graph
4. Use G-Solv > Max or Min to find the points

calculator.description: Graph the function to visually confirm the maximum and minimum points.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Based on the Second Derivative Test:

- There is a **local maximum** at $x = 1$
- There is a **local minimum** at $x = 3$

steps.title: Identify derivative and critical points
steps.content:
To apply the **First Derivative Test**, we first need the gradient function $f'(x)$ and the critical points where $f'(x) = 0$.

From the previous parts or by differentiating $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 1$:
$$f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9$$

Setting $f'(x) = 0$ gives the critical points:
$$3(x^2 - 4x + 3) = 0 \Rightarrow 3(x - 1)(x - 3) = 0$$

So the critical points are at $x = 1$ and $x = 3$.

steps.title: Set up sign diagram intervals
steps.content:
We divide the number line into intervals based on our critical points $x=1$ and $x=3$. We need to test the sign of the gradient $f'(x)$ in each interval to see if the function is increasing (positive slope) or decreasing (negative slope).

**Intervals to test:**
1. $x < 1$
2. $1 < x < 3$
3. $x > 3$

steps.title: Test left of x = 1
steps.content:
Choose a test value where $x < 1$, for example, $x = 0$.
Substitute this into $f'(x)$:

$$f'(0) = 3(0)^2 - 12(0) + 9 = 9$$

Since $f'(0) > 0$, the gradient is **positive** ($+$). The graph is **increasing** $\nearrow$.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate 3x^2-12x+9 at x=0

steps.title: Test between x = 1 and x = 3
steps.content:
Choose a test value where $1 < x < 3$, for example, $x = 2$.
Substitute this into $f'(x)$:

$$f'(2) = 3(2)^2 - 12(2) + 9 = 12 - 24 + 9 = -3$$

Since $f'(2) < 0$, the gradient is **negative** ($-$). The graph is **decreasing** $\searrow$.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate 3x^2-12x+9 at x=2

steps.title: Test right of x = 3
steps.content:
Choose a test value where $x > 3$, for example, $x = 4$.
Substitute this into $f'(x)$:

$$f'(4) = 3(4)^2 - 12(4) + 9 = 48 - 48 + 9 = 9$$

Since $f'(4) > 0$, the gradient is **positive** ($+$). The graph is **increasing** $\nearrow$.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate 3x^2-12x+9 at x=4

steps.title: Classify critical point at x = 1
steps.content:
Analyze the change in slope around $x = 1$:
- Left ($x < 1$): Positive ($+$)
- Right ($x > 1$): Negative ($-$)

The slope changes from increasing to decreasing ($+ \to -$). This shape $\cap$ corresponds to a peak.

Therefore, there is a **local maximum** at $x = 1$.

steps.title: Classify critical point at x = 3
steps.content:
Analyze the change in slope around $x = 3$:
- Left ($x < 3$): Negative ($-$)
- Right ($x > 3$): Positive ($+$)

The slope changes from decreasing to increasing ($- \to +$). This shape $\cup$ corresponds to a valley.

Therefore, there is a **local minimum** at $x = 3$.

parts.content: Determine the **coordinates** of the local maximum and local minimum points.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute $x = 3$ into $f(x)$:
  $f(3) = 3^3 - 6(3)^2 + 9(3) + 1 = 27 - 54 + 27 + 1 = 1$
  The local minimum point is $(3, 1)$

- Substitute $x = 1$ into $f(x)$:
  $f(1) = 1^3 - 6(1)^2 + 9(1) + 1 = 1 - 6 + 9 + 1 = 5$
  The local maximum point is $(1, 5)$

- State the coordinates: $(3, 1)$ and $(1, 5)$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Find the derivative f'(x)
steps.content:
To find the stationary points (which include local maxima and minima), we first need to differentiate $f(x)$ with respect to $x$. We use the power rule from the data booklet.

$$f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 1$$

$$f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9$$

steps.title: Solve for critical values
steps.content:
Set the derivative equal to zero to find the critical values where the gradient is horizontal.

$$\begin{aligned} 3x^2 - 12x + 9 &= 0 \\ 3(x^2 - 4x + 3) &= 0 \\ x^2 - 4x + 3 &= 0 \\ (x - 3)(x - 1) &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the critical values are $x = 1$ and $x = 3$.

gdc.instructions: Go to Equation > Polynomial > Degree 2. Enter coefficients 3, -12, 9. Press Solve (F1).

ti84.instructions: Press [APPS] > PlySmlt2 > Root Finder. Enter Order 2. Input coefficients a=3, b=-12, c=9. Solve.

desmos.instructions: Type 3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 0 to see the vertical lines at x=1 and x=3.

calculator.description: Find the roots of the quadratic equation

steps.title: Classify using second derivative
steps.content:
We classify these points using the second derivative test. First, find $f''(x)$:

$$f''(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(3x^2 - 12x + 9) = 6x - 12$$

Now substitute the critical values:

*   **At $x = 1$:**
    $$f''(1) = 6(1) - 12 = -6$$
    Since $f''(1) < 0$, the curve is concave down, so this is a **local maximum**.

*   **At $x = 3$:**
    $$f''(3) = 6(3) - 12 = 6$$
    Since $f''(3) > 0$, the curve is concave up, so this is a **local minimum**.

steps.title: Calculate coordinates
steps.content:
Substitute the $x$-values back into the original function $f(x)$ to find the corresponding $y$-coordinates.

For the **local maximum** at $x = 1$:
$$f(1) = 1^3 - 6(1)^2 + 9(1) + 1 = 1 - 6 + 9 + 1 = 5$$
So, the local maximum is $(1, 5)$.

For the **local minimum** at $x = 3$:
$$f(3) = 3^3 - 6(3)^2 + 9(3) + 1 = 27 - 54 + 27 + 1 = 1$$
So, the local minimum is $(3, 1)$.

steps.content:
The coordinates are:

*   **Local maximum:** $(1, 5)$
*   **Local minimum:** $(3, 1)$

questions.specification:
A particle moves along a straight line such that its displacement, $s(t)$ meters, from a fixed point O at time $t$ seconds is given by the function $$s(t) = t^3 - 6t^2 + 9t + 2$$

The following diagram shows the graph of the displacement function.

![Graph of displacement s(t) against time t. The curve starts at (0, 2), rises to a local maximum at (1, 6), falls to a local minimum at (3, 2), and rises again passing through (4, 6).](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/e09676dd-e2df-4674-af27-202a030c045c.png)
