questions.specification:
A large reservoir initially contains pure water. Water containing salt begins to flow into the reservoir. The solution is kept uniform by stirring and leaves the reservoir through an outlet at its base. Let $x$ grams represent the amount of salt in the reservoir and let $t$ minutes represent the time since the salt water began flowing into the reservoir.

The rate of change of the amount of salt in the reservoir, $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$, is described by the differential equation $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - \frac{x}{t + 1}$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > AHL 5.18—1st order DE’s – Euler method, variables separable, integrating factor, homogeneous DE using sub y=vx > Question Type 4: Solving with the integrating factor (linear ODEs)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 4: Solving with the integrating factor (linear ODEs)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The differential equation is linear first-order and parts (a) and (b) explicitly require using an integrating factor to solve it.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > SL 5.8—Testing for max and min, optimisation. Points of inflexion > Question Type 1: Finding the stationary points of specific functions by setting first derivatives to 0
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Finding the stationary points of specific functions by setting first derivatives to 0
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) asks for when the amount of salt is decreasing most rapidly, which involves optimizing a rate function via stationary points (typically using derivatives).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > SL 5.11—Definite integrals, areas under curve onto x-axis and areas between curves > Question Type 1: Evaluating definite integrals analytically
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Evaluating definite integrals analytically
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) asks for the amount of salt that left during the first 60 minutes, which is found by integrating the outflow rate x/(t+1) over t∈[0,60], i.e., evaluating a definite integral analytically.

parts.content: Show that $t + 1$ is an integrating factor for this differential equation.
parts.markscheme:
### Method #1

- Integrating factor formula: $I(t) = \text{e}^{\int P(t) \,\text{d}t}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- $\text{e}^{\int \frac{1}{t + 1} \,\text{d}t}$

- $= \text{e}^{\ln(t + 1)}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- $= t + 1$

### Method #2

- Attempt product rule differentiation on $\frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}(x(t + 1))$ <marks>M1</marks>

- $\frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}(x(t + 1)) = \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}(t + 1) + x$

- $= (t + 1)(\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + \frac{x}{t + 1})$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Therefore, $t + 1$ is an integrating factor for this differential equation

<marks>2 marks total</marks>

steps.title: Rearrange into standard form
steps.content:
To find the integrating factor, we first write the differential equation in the standard linear form:
$$ \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + P(t)x = Q(t) $$

The given equation is:
$$ \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - \frac{x}{t + 1} $$

Add $\frac{x}{t+1}$ to both sides to group the $x$ terms:
$$ \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + \frac{1}{t+1}x = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} $$

steps.title: Identify the function P(t)
steps.content:
Comparing our rearranged equation to the standard form $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + P(t)x = Q(t)$, we identify the coefficient of $x$:
$$ P(t) = \frac{1}{t+1} $$

steps.title: Apply integrating factor formula
steps.content:
The formula for the integrating factor $I(t)$ is:
$$ I(t) = \text{e}^{\int P(t) \,\text{d}t} $$

Substituting our function for $P(t)$:
$$ I(t) = \text{e}^{\int \frac{1}{t+1} \,\text{d}t} $$

steps.title: Evaluate the integral
steps.content:
We evaluate the integral in the exponent. Using the standard integral (derived from the derivative of $\ln x$ in the data booklet):
$$ \int \frac{1}{t+1} \,\text{d}t = \ln(t+1) $$

So the integrating factor becomes:
$$ I(t) = \text{e}^{\ln(t+1)} $$

Note: We assume $t+1 > 0$ since $t$ represents time, so absolute value signs are not required.

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.content:
Using the logarithmic property that exponentials and natural logarithms are inverse functions ($\text{e}^{\ln u} = u$):
$$ I(t) = t+1 $$

Thus, we have shown that $t+1$ is the integrating factor.

steps.title: Differentiate the product
steps.content:
To verify the integrating factor, we start by differentiating the product $x(t + 1)$ with respect to $t$ using the product rule.

Using the rule $\frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}(uv) = u\frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}t} + v\frac{\text{d}u}{\text{d}t}$ with $u=x$ and $v=(t+1)$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}(x(t + 1)) &= x \cdot \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}(t + 1) + (t + 1) \cdot \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} \\ &= x(1) + (t + 1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} \\ &= x + (t + 1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Rearrange the differential equation
steps.content:
Next, we rearrange the given differential equation to group the $x$ terms on the left-hand side.

Starting with:
$$\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - \frac{x}{t + 1}$$

Add $\frac{x}{t + 1}$ to both sides:
$$\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + \frac{x}{t + 1} = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$$

steps.title: Multiply by the factor
steps.content:
Now, multiply the entire rearranged equation by the factor $(t + 1)$ to see if it matches our product derivative.

$$(t + 1)\left[\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + \frac{x}{t + 1}\right] = (t + 1)\left[10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}\right]$$

Distributing $(t + 1)$ on the left side:
$$(t + 1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + x = 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$$ 

Notice that the left-hand side is exactly $x + (t + 1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$.

steps.title: Verify and conclude
steps.content:
Comparing the results from Step 0 and Step 2:

1. Derivative of product: $$x + (t + 1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$$
2. Modified differential equation: $$(t + 1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + x$$

Since multiplying the differential equation by $(t + 1)$ transforms the left-hand side into the exact derivative of $x(t + 1)$, we have shown that $t + 1$ is an integrating factor.

parts.content: Hence, by solving this differential equation, show that $x(t) = \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5)}{t + 1}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempting to multiply through by $(t + 1)$ and rearrange <marks>M1</marks>

- $(t + 1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + x = 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- $\frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\left(x(t + 1)\right) = 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$

- $x(t + 1) = \int{10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}}\,\text{d}t$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Attempting to integrate the RHS by parts <marks>M1</marks>

- $= -40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + 40\int{\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}}\,\text{d}t$

- $= -40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - 160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + C$ <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Condone the absence of $C$.</note>

### Method #1

- Substituting $t = 0, x = 0 \Rightarrow C = 200$ <marks>M1</marks>

- $x = \frac{-40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - 160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + 200}{t + 1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Using $-40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$ as the highest common factor of $-40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$ and $-160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$ <marks>M1</marks>

### Method #2

- Using $-40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$ as the highest common factor of $-40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$ and $-160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$ giving

  $x(t + 1) = -40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5) + C$ <marks>M1A1</marks>

- Substituting $t = 0, x = 0 \Rightarrow C = 200$ <marks>M1</marks>

### Final step for both methods

- $x(t) = \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5)}{t + 1}$ (Answer Given)

<marks>8 marks total</marks>

steps.title: Rewrite in linear form
steps.content:
First, we identify that this is a first-order linear differential equation of the form $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + P(t)x = Q(t)$.

We rearrange the given equation by moving the term with $x$ to the left-hand side:

$$\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + \frac{1}{t + 1}x = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$$ 

Here, we can identify $P(t) = \frac{1}{t+1}$ and $Q(t) = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$.

steps.title: Calculate integrating factor
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the integrating factor, $I(t)$, using the formula $I(t) = \text{e}^{\int P(t) \text{d}t}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Integrating Factor} &= \text{e}^{\int \frac{1}{t + 1} \,\text{d}t} \\ &= \text{e}^{\ln(t + 1)} \\ &= t + 1 \end{aligned}$$

(Note: Since $t$ represents time, $t \ge 0$, so $t+1 > 0$ and we do not need absolute value signs).

steps.title: Multiply by integrating factor
steps.content:
We multiply the entire differential equation by the integrating factor $(t + 1)$:

$$(t + 1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + x = 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$$

We recognize that the left-hand side is the derivative of the product $x(t+1)$. We can rewrite the equation as:

$$\frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\left[x(t + 1)\right] = 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$$ 

Integrating both sides with respect to $t$ gives:

$$x(t + 1) = \int 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \,\text{d}t$$

steps.title: Integrate by parts
steps.content:
To solve the integral on the right-hand side, we use **integration by parts**. The formula is $\int u \frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}x} \text{d}x = uv - \int v \frac{\text{d}u}{\text{d}x} \text{d}x$.

Let:
$$\begin{aligned} u &= 10(t + 1) & \frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}t} &= \text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \\ \frac{\text{d}u}{\text{d}t} &= 10 & v &= -4\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \end{aligned}$$

Applying the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} \int 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \,\text{d}t &= [10(t + 1)][-4\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}] - \int (-4\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}})(10) \,\text{d}t \\ &= -40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + 40\int \text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \,\text{d}t \\ &= -40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + 40(-4\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}) + C \\ &= -40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - 160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + C \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: General solution
steps.content:
Now we equate our integrated result back to the left-hand side from Step 2:

$$x(t + 1) = -40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - 160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + C$$

To simplify the right-hand side, we can factor out $-40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$ from the first two terms:

$$\begin{aligned} -40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - 160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} &= -40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}[(t + 1) + 4] \\ &= -40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5) \end{aligned}$$

So the general solution is:
$$x(t + 1) = -40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5) + C$$

steps.title: Find constant C
steps.content:
We use the initial condition to find $C$. The reservoir initially contains pure water, meaning when $t = 0$, the amount of salt $x = 0$.

Substituting $t = 0$ and $x = 0$ into our equation:

$$\begin{aligned} 0(0 + 1) &= -40\text{e}^{0}(0 + 5) + C \\ 0 &= -40(1)(5) + C \\ 0 &= -200 + C \\ C &= 200 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value of C:

ti84.instructions: Calculate the value of C:

desmos.instructions: Calculate the value of C:

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
Finally, we substitute $C = 200$ back into the general solution and solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} x(t + 1) &= -40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5) + 200 \\ x(t + 1) &= 200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5) \end{aligned}$$

Dividing by $(t + 1)$ gives the required expression:

$$x(t) = \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5)}{t + 1}$$

steps.title: Analyze the problem
steps.content:
The problem asks us to solve a first-order differential equation to find an expression for the amount of salt $x$ at time $t$. 

We are given:
1. The differential equation: $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - \frac{x}{t + 1}$
2. Initial conditions: The reservoir initially contains pure water, meaning there is no salt at the start.

This involves concepts from **AHL 5.18—1st order DE’s**.

steps.title: Rearrange and clear denominator
steps.content:
First, we rearrange the equation to group the $x$ and $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$ terms on the left side:

$$ \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + \frac{x}{t + 1} = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} $$

To apply the **Reverse Product Rule by Inspection**, we clear the denominator by multiplying the entire equation by $(t+1)$:

$$ (t+1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + x = 10(t+1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} $$

steps.title: Recognize the derivative
steps.content:
By inspecting the left-hand side, $(t+1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + x$, we can recognize this pattern as the derivative of the product $x(t+1)$.

Using the Product Rule $\frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}(uv) = u\frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}t} + v\frac{\text{d}u}{\text{d}t}$ with $u=x$ and $v=t+1$:

$$ \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\big(x(t+1)\big) = x(1) + (t+1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} $$

This matches our equation exactly. We can now rewrite the differential equation as:

$$ \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\big(x(t + 1)\big) = 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} $$

steps.title: Integrate both sides
steps.content:
Now we integrate both sides with respect to $t$. The left side simplifies directly:

$$ x(t + 1) = \int 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \,\text{d}t $$

steps.title: Apply Integration by Parts
steps.content:
To solve the integral on the right, we use **Integration by Parts** (**AHL 5.16**). The formula is:

$$ \int u \frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}x} \text{d}x = uv - \int v \frac{\text{d}u}{\text{d}x} \text{d}x $$

Let:
$$\begin{aligned} u &= 10(t+1) & \frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}t} &= \text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \\ \frac{\text{d}u}{\text{d}t} &= 10 & v &= -4\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \end{aligned}$$

Applying the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} \int 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \,\text{d}t &= 10(t+1)(-4\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}) - \int (-4\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}})(10) \,\text{d}t \\ &= -40(t+1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + 40 \int \text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \,\text{d}t \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve the integral
steps.content:
Completing the integration:

$$\begin{aligned} \int 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \,\text{d}t &= -40(t+1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + 40(-4\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}) + C \\ &= -40(t+1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - 160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + C \end{aligned}$$

So our general solution is:

$$ x(t + 1) = -40(t+1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - 160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + C $$

steps.title: Find the constant C
steps.content:
We use the initial condition to find $C$. The problem states the reservoir initially contains pure water, so when $t = 0$, $x = 0$.

Substituting these values:

$$\begin{aligned} 0(0 + 1) &= -40(0+1)\text{e}^{0} - 160\text{e}^{0} + C \\ 0 &= -40(1)(1) - 160(1) + C \\ 0 &= -40 - 160 + C \\ 0 &= -200 + C \\ C &= 200 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the numerical value

ti84.instructions: Calculate the numerical value

desmos.instructions: Calculate the numerical value

calculator.description: Calculate C by evaluating the RHS at t=0

steps.title: Format the final answer
steps.content:
Now we substitute $C = 200$ back into the equation and solve for $x$:

$$ x(t + 1) = 200 - 40(t+1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - 160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} $$

Factor out $-40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$ from the last two terms to simplify:

$$\begin{aligned} x(t + 1) &= 200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \big((t+1) + 4\big) \\ x(t + 1) &= 200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5) \end{aligned}$$

Finally, divide by $(t + 1)$ to get the required form:

$$ x(t) = \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5)}{t + 1} $$

parts.content: Find the value of $t$ at which the amount of salt in the reservoir is decreasing most rapidly.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize that we need to find the minimum value of $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Set up the equation for the second derivative:

  $\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = -\frac{5}{2}\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + \frac{x}{(t+1)^2} - \frac{1}{t+1}\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$

- Set $\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = 0$ and solve for $t$

- Or, graph $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$ and find the minimum point

- The amount of salt is decreasing most rapidly at $t = 12.9$ minutes <marks>A1</marks>

<marks>2 marks total</marks>

steps.title: Identify the optimization condition
steps.content:
To find the time when the amount of salt is **decreasing most rapidly**, we need to find the minimum value of the rate of change $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$.

In calculus, finding a minimum or maximum involves finding where the derivative is zero. Therefore, we must find where the second derivative is zero:

$$\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = 0$$

steps.title: Differentiate the rate equation
steps.content:
We are given the differential equation:

$$\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - \frac{x}{t + 1}$$

We differentiate this with respect to $t$ to find an expression for $\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2}$. Using the chain rule for the first term and the quotient rule for the second term (where $x$ is a function of $t$):

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} &= \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\left(10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}\right) - \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\left(\frac{x}{t+1}\right) \\ &= 10\left(-\frac{1}{4}\right)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - \left(\frac{(t+1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} - x(1)}{(t+1)^2}\right) \\ &= -\frac{5}{2}\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - \frac{1}{t+1}\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + \frac{x}{(t+1)^2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Set up the equation to solve
steps.content:
To find the minimum rate, we set the second derivative to zero:

$$-\frac{5}{2}\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - \frac{1}{t+1}\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + \frac{x}{(t+1)^2} = 0$$

To solve this for $t$, we need the expression for $x$ in terms of $t$. From the earlier parts of the question (solving the linear differential equation with initial condition $x(0)=0$), the expression for $x$ is:

$$x = \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t+5)}{t+1}$$

steps.title: Solve for t using a calculator
steps.content:
We can now solve for $t$ by graphing the derivative of the rate function, or by using the equation solver on a GDC. We are looking for the root of the second derivative function.

The amount of salt is decreasing most rapidly at:

$$t = 12.9 \text{ minutes}$$

gdc.instructions:

2. Enter y2 = d(y1)/dx
3. Enter y3 = d(y2)/dx
4. Plot y3 and find the root (x-intercept).

calculator.description: Find the value of t where the second derivative is zero

steps.title: Interpret the question
steps.content:
The question asks for the time when the amount of salt is **decreasing most rapidly**. 

Since $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$ represents the rate of change of salt:
- A negative value of $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$ indicates the amount is decreasing.
- "Most rapidly" means we are looking for the **minimum value** (the most negative value) of this rate.

Therefore, we need to find the minimum point of the function $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$.

steps.title: Retrieve the expression for x(t)
steps.content:
To graph the rate $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$ on a calculator, we need it purely in terms of $t$. The given equation contains $x$:

$$\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - \frac{x}{t + 1}$$

This implies we must use the solution for $x(t)$ found in previous parts (Part 1 or 2). Solving the linear differential equation with the initial condition $x(0)=0$ yields:

$$x(t) = \frac{200 - 40(t+5)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}}{t+1}$$

steps.title: Define the function to graph
steps.content:
Substitute the expression for $x(t)$ into the rate equation to create a single function of $t$ that we can graph:

$$f(t) = 10\text{e}^{-0.25t} - \frac{\left(\frac{200 - 40(t+5)\text{e}^{-0.25t}}{t+1}\right)}{t + 1}$$

We will plot this function and find its minimum.

gdc.instructions:

2. Define f2(x) = 10*e^(-0.25*x) - f1(x)/(x+1)
3. Plot f2(x) and adjust the window to view the minimum.

3. Enter the rate function into Y2 using Y1:
   Y2 = 10*e^(-0.25*X) - Y1 / (X+1)
4. Press [WINDOW] and set Xmin=0, Xmax=20, Ymin=-2, Ymax=2 to see the curve.
5. Press [GRAPH].

desmos.instructions:
1. Define x_salt(t) = (200 - 40*(t+5)*exp(-0.25*t))/(t+1)
2. Define rate(t) = 10*exp(-0.25*t) - x_salt(t)/(t+1)
3. Graph rate(t) and click the curve to find the minimum point.

Using your calculator's minimum finding tool:

3. The calculator will identify the coordinates of the minimum point.

The minimum occurs at approximately:
$$x = 12.924...$$
$$y = -0.490...$$

Here, the $x$-coordinate on the graph represents our time $t$.

The question asks for the value of $t$. Rounding our result to 3 significant figures:

$$t = 12.9 \text{ minutes}$$

parts.content:
The rate of change of the amount of salt leaving the reservoir is equal to $\frac{x}{t + 1}$.

Find the amount of salt that left the reservoir during the first 60 minutes.
parts.markscheme:
### Method #1

- Forming an integral representing the amount of salt that left the reservoir: <marks>M1</marks>

  $\int_{0}^{60} \frac{x(t)}{t + 1} \,\text{d}t$

- Correct substitution of $x(t)$: <marks>A1</marks>

  $\int_{0}^{60} \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}\left( t + 5 \right)}{\left( t + 1 \right)^2} \,\text{d}t$

- Final answer: 36.7 (grams) <marks>A2</marks>

### Method #2

- Forming an integral representing the amount of salt that entered the reservoir minus the amount of salt in the reservoir at $t = 60$ (minutes): <marks>A1</marks>

  $\int_{0}^{60} 10 \text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}\,\text{d}t - x(60)$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Final answer: 36.7 (grams) <marks>A2</marks>

<marks>4 marks total</marks>

steps.title: Identify the output rate
steps.content:
To find the total amount of salt leaving the reservoir, we first identify the rate at which salt leaves. The question explicitly gives this rate.

Rate of salt leaving = $$\frac{x}{t + 1}$$

highlights.text: The rate of change of the amount of salt leaving the reservoir is equal to $\frac{x}{t + 1}$

steps.title: Set up the definite integral
steps.content:
The total amount of salt that left is the integral of the leaving rate over the time interval from $t = 0$ to $t = 60$. We set up the definite integral:

$$ \text{Amount left} = \int_{0}^{60} \frac{x(t)}{t + 1} \,\text{d}t $$

steps.title: Substitute the expression for x(t)
steps.content:
From the previous part of the question (solving the differential equation), the expression for the amount of salt $x(t)$ is:

$$ x(t) = \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5)}{t + 1} $$

Substitute this expression into the integral. Note that dividing by $(t+1)$ again will square the denominator:

$$ \int_{0}^{60} \frac{1}{t+1} \left( \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5)}{t + 1} \right) \,\text{d}t = \int_{0}^{60} \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5)}{(t + 1)^2} \,\text{d}t $$

steps.title: Evaluate using a calculator
steps.content: Use the numerical integration feature of your GDC to evaluate this integral.

gdc.instructions:
In Run-Matrix mode, select OPTN > CALC > ∫dx.
Enter the expression (200 - 40e^(-x/4)(x+5)) / (x+1)^2.
Set lower bound 0 and upper bound 60.

ti84.instructions:
Press MATH > 9 (fnInt).
Enter the integrand: (200 - 40*e^(-X/4)*(X+5)) / (X+1)^2
Integrate with respect to X from 0 to 60.

desmos.instructions:
Type 'int' to create an integral symbol.
Set bounds 0 to 60.
Enter the integrand.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator gives a value of approximately $36.702...$ grams. Round this to 3 significant figures.

Amount of salt left $$\approx 36.7 \text{ grams}$$

steps.title: Method Strategy
steps.content:
To find the amount of salt that left the reservoir, we use the conservation principle (Input minus Accumulation). This states that the total amount of salt that leaves is equal to the total amount that entered minus the amount remaining in the reservoir at time $t=60$:

$$\text{Total Out} = \text{Total In} - \text{Amount Remaining}$$

steps.title: Calculate Salt Entered
steps.content:
The rate at which salt enters is the first term of the differential equation, $10\text{e}^{-t/4}$. We integrate this rate from $t=0$ to $t=60$ to find the total salt input:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Total In} &= \int_{0}^{60} 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \,\text{d}t \\ &= \left[ -40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \right]_{0}^{60} \\ &= -40\text{e}^{-15} - (-40\text{e}^{0}) \\ &= 40(1 - \text{e}^{-15}) \\ &\approx 40 \text{ grams} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In Run-Matrix, press OPTN > CALC > ∫dx. Enter ∫(10e^(-x/4), 0, 60)

ti84.instructions: Press MATH > 9:fnInt. Enter fnInt(10*e^(-X/4), X, 0, 60)

desmos.instructions: Type int(0, 60, 10e^(-t/4) dt)

calculator.description: Calculate the definite integral for salt input

steps.title: Find Salt Remaining x(60)
steps.content:
To find the salt remaining, $x(60)$, we solve the differential equation $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + \frac{x}{t + 1} = 10\text{e}^{-t/4}$. Using the integrating factor $(t+1)$, the solution is:

$$x(t) = \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5)}{t + 1}$$

Substituting $t=60$:

$$\begin{aligned} x(60) &= \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-15}(65)}{61} \\ &\approx \frac{200}{61} \\ &\approx 3.28 \text{ grams} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate Final Answer
steps.content:
Finally, subtract the amount remaining from the total amount entered:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Salt Left} &= \text{Total In} - x(60) \\ &= 40(1 - \text{e}^{-15}) - 3.28 \\ &= 40 - 3.28 \\ &= 36.7 \text{ grams} \end{aligned}$$

questions.specification: Consider the function $f(x) = 4x^{2y}-5x^y+1$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Number and Algebra > SL 1.7—Laws of exponents and logs > Question Type 3: Applying indices and negative exponents
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Applying indices and negative exponents
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) sets f(3)=0, giving an equation in powers like 3^{2y} and 3^y; solving relies on laws of indices (e.g., rewriting 3^{2y}=(3^y)^2) and handling exponents.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > SL 5.8—Testing for max and min, optimisation. Points of inflexion > Question Type 1: Finding the stationary points of specific functions by setting first derivatives to 0
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Finding the stationary points of specific functions by setting first derivatives to 0
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) asks for x such that f'(x)=0 (with x>0), i.e., finding stationary points by differentiating and setting the first derivative equal to zero.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > SL 5.6—Differentiating polynomials n E Q. Chain, product and quotient rules > Question Type 1: Finding the derivatives of more complex functions that involve trigonometric, exponential functions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Finding the derivatives of more complex functions that involve trigonometric, exponential functions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Differentiating f(x)=4x^{2y}-5x^y+1 (with parameter y) requires using power-rule-style differentiation for non-integer/parameter exponents, then simplifying.

parts.content: Find for what values of $y$ is $f(3)=0$.
parts.markscheme:
- Setting $f(3)=4(3^{2y})-5(3^y)+1=0$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Noticing it creates a quadratic with $u=3^y$ hence solving $4u^2-5u+1$ using quadratic equation <marks>M1</marks>
- Therefore $u=\frac{5\pm\sqrt{25-4(4)}}{8}=\frac{5\pm3}{8}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- For $u=\frac{5+3}{8}=1$ we have $3^y=1$ hence $y=0$ <marks>A1</marks>
- For $u=\frac{5-3}{8}=\frac{1}{4}$ we have $3^y=\frac{1}{4}$ hence $y=\log_3\frac{1}{4}$ <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

steps.title: Substitute x = 3
steps.content:
The question asks for the values of $y$ such that $f(3)=0$. First, we substitute $x=3$ into the given function $f(x) = 4x^{2y}-5x^y+1$ and equate it to zero:

$$4(3)^{2y} - 5(3)^y + 1 = 0$$

steps.title: Apply quadratic substitution
steps.content:
This equation contains powers of 3. Using the law of exponents $(a^m)^n = a^{mn}$, we can rewrite $3^{2y}$ as $(3^y)^2$. This reveals that the equation is quadratic in form.

We define a new variable $u$:
$$u = 3^y$$

Substituting this into our equation gives the quadratic equation:
$$4u^2 - 5u + 1 = 0$$

steps.title: Solve the quadratic equation
steps.content:
We can solve $4u^2 - 5u + 1 = 0$ by factoring or using the **quadratic formula** from the data booklet with $a=4$, $b=-5$, and $c=1$.

$$x=\frac{-b\pm\sqrt{b^{2}-4ac}}{2a}$$

Substituting our values (solving for $u$ instead of $x$):

$$\begin{aligned} u &= \frac{-(-5) \pm \sqrt{(-5)^2 - 4(4)(1)}}{2(4)} \\ u &= \frac{5 \pm \sqrt{25 - 16}}{8} \\ u &= \frac{5 \pm \sqrt{9}}{8} \\ u &= \frac{5 \pm 3}{8} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Equation menu
2. Select Polynomial (F2)
3. Select Degree 2
4. Enter a=4, b=-5, c=1
5. Press SOLVE

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [APPS] and select PlySmlt2
2. Select 'Polynomial Root Finder'
3. Set Order to 2
4. Enter coefficients: a2=4, a1=-5, a0=1
5. Press [SOLVE]

desmos.instructions: Type the equation 4x^2 - 5x + 1 = 0 to see the vertical lines at the roots, or graph y = 4x^2 - 5x + 1 and click the x-intercepts.

calculator.description: Find the roots of the quadratic equation 4u^2 - 5u + 1 = 0

steps.title: Identify solutions for u
steps.content:
Calculating the two possible values for $u$:

$$u_1 = \frac{5+3}{8} = \frac{8}{8} = 1$$

$$u_2 = \frac{5-3}{8} = \frac{2}{8} = \frac{1}{4}$$

steps.title: Solve for y (Case 1)
steps.content:
Now we substitute back $u = 3^y$ to find $y$. For the first solution $u=1$:

$$3^y = 1$$

Since any non-zero number to the power of 0 is 1:
$$y = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for y (Case 2)
steps.content:
For the second solution $u = \frac{1}{4}$:

$$3^y = \frac{1}{4}$$

Using the definition of logarithms ($x = a^y \iff y = \log_a x$):

$$y = \log_3\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)$$

steps.title: State final answers
steps.content:
The two values for $y$ are:

$$y = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad y = \log_3\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)$$

parts.content: Find the value of $x$ for which $f'(x)=0$, given that $x > 0$ and $y \neq 0$.
parts.markscheme:
- Applying power rule to find derivative: $f'(x)=8yx^{2y-1}-5yx^{y-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Setting $f'(x)=0$ such that $8yx^{2y-1}=5yx^{y-1}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Dividing by $x^{y-1}$ (since $x>0$) and $y$ (since $y\neq0$): $8x^y - 5 = 0$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Hence $x^y=\frac{5}{8}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Therefore $x=\left(\frac{5}{8}\right)^{\frac{1}{y}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

steps.title: Differentiate using the power rule
steps.content:
To find the stationary points, we first need to find the derivative of the function $f(x) = 4x^{2y}-5x^y+1$ with respect to $x$.

Using the power rule from the data booklet ($f(x)=x^{n} \Rightarrow f'(x)=nx^{n-1}$), we differentiate each term separately treating $y$ as a constant:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d}{dx}(4x^{2y}) &= 4 \cdot 2y \cdot x^{2y-1} = 8yx^{2y-1} \\ \frac{d}{dx}(-5x^y) &= -5 \cdot y \cdot x^{y-1} = -5yx^{y-1} \\ \frac{d}{dx}(1) &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

Combining these terms gives the derivative:

$$f'(x) = 8yx^{2y-1} - 5yx^{y-1}$$

steps.title: Set the derivative to zero
steps.content:
We need to find the value of $x$ for which $f'(x) = 0$. We set our expression for the derivative equal to zero:

$$8yx^{2y-1} - 5yx^{y-1} = 0$$

steps.title: Factor the equation
steps.content:
To solve for $x$, we look for common factors. Both terms contain $y$ and powers of $x$.

Notice that $x^{2y-1}$ can be written as $x^y \cdot x^{y-1}$. This reveals a common factor of $yx^{y-1}$ in both terms.

$$\begin{aligned} 8y(x^y \cdot x^{y-1}) - 5yx^{y-1} &= 0 \\ yx^{y-1}(8x^y - 5) &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Isolate the term involving x
steps.content:
We have a product of two terms equaling zero: $yx^{y-1}$ and $(8x^y - 5)$.

The question states that $x > 0$ and $y \neq 0$. This implies that $yx^{y-1} \neq 0$. Therefore, the other factor must be zero:

$$8x^y - 5 = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
Now we rearrange to solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} 8x^y &= 5 \\ x^y &= \frac{5}{8} \end{aligned}$$

To isolate $x$, we raise both sides to the power of $\frac{1}{y}$:

$$x = \left(\frac{5}{8}\right)^{\frac{1}{y}}$$ 

This matches the final requirement for the solution.

steps.title: Apply substitution variable
steps.content:
To simplify the function, we can use the substitution method. Notice that the term $x^{2y}$ can be written as $(x^y)^2$.

Let $u = x^y$. Substituting this into the original function converts it into a quadratic equation:

$$f(x) = 4(x^y)^2 - 5(x^y) + 1 \Rightarrow 4u^2 - 5u + 1$$

steps.title: Find the vertex of the quadratic
steps.content:
Since $x > 0$ and $y \neq 0$, the derivative of the inner function $u=x^y$ is never zero. Therefore, $f'(x) = 0$ occurs exactly when the derivative of the quadratic with respect to $u$ is zero.

This corresponds to the vertex (axis of symmetry) of the quadratic $4u^2 - 5u + 1$. We use the axis of symmetry formula from the data booklet:

$$u = -\frac{b}{2a}$$

steps.title: Calculate the value of u
steps.content:
Substitute the coefficients $a = 4$ and $b = -5$ into the formula to find the value of $u$ where the gradient is zero:

$$u = -\frac{-5}{2(4)} = \frac{5}{8}$$

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
Now substitute $u = x^y$ back into the equation to solve for $x$:

$$x^y = \frac{5}{8}$$

To isolate $x$, we take the $y$-th root of both sides (or raise both sides to the power of $\frac{1}{y}$):

$$x = \left(\frac{5}{8}\right)^{\frac{1}{y}}$$

parts.content: Find the critical points of the function $f(x)$ and determine their nature (local maximum, local minimum, or stationary point of inflection).
parts.markscheme:
- Find the derivative: $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Set the derivative equal to zero and solve for $x$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
3x^2 - 12x + 9 &= 0 \implies x^2 - 4x + 3 = 0 \\
(x-3)(x-1) &= 0 \implies x = 1, x = 3
\end{aligned}
$$
<marks>A1</marks>

- To determine the nature of the critical points, use the second derivative test or a sign chart: <marks>M1</marks>
  
  **Method A (Second Derivative):**
  $f''(x) = 6x - 12$
  At $x = 1$, $f''(1) = -6 < 0$ (local maximum)
  At $x = 3$, $f''(3) = 6 > 0$ (local minimum)

  **Method B (First Derivative/Sign Chart):**
  For $x < 1$, $f'(x) > 0$ and for $1 < x < 3$, $f'(x) < 0$ (local maximum at $x=1$)
  For $1 < x < 3$, $f'(x) < 0$ and for $x > 3$, $f'(x) > 0$ (local minimum at $x=3$)

- Local maximum at $x = 1$, Local minimum at $x = 3$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Determine the inflection points of the function $f(x)$.
parts.markscheme:
- Set $f''(x) = 0$ and solve:
  $6x - 12 = 0 \implies x = 2$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Confirm change of concavity/sign of $f''(x)$ around $x = 2$:
  For $x < 2$: $f''(x) < 0$ (concave down)
  For $x > 2$: $f''(x) > 0$ (concave up)

- Inflection point at $x = 2$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

questions.specification: A rectangular piece of cardboard with dimensions 20 cm by 30 cm is to be made into an open box by cutting squares of side length $x$ cm from each corner and folding up the sides.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > SL 5.8—Testing for max and min, optimisation. Points of inflexion > Question Type 3: Working with shapes to find the largest (or smallest) area (or volume) of specific 2D (or 3D) shapes
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Working with shapes to find the largest (or smallest) area (or volume) of specific 2D (or 3D) shapes
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The task is a classic optimisation-with-geometry problem: form V(x) for an open box from a 20×30 sheet, then maximize volume by differentiating and solving for x, and compute the maximum volume.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > SL 5.7—The second derivative > Question Type 1: Calculating the second derivatives of functions involving less rules
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Calculating the second derivatives of functions involving less rules

parts.content: Find the volume $V$ of the box in terms of $x$.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify dimensions after cutting and folding:
  Length: $(30 - 2x)$
  Width: $(20 - 2x)$
  Height: $x$ <marks>A1</marks>

- State correct expression for the volume:
  $V = x(30 - 2x)(20 - 2x)$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Expansion of the expression:
  $V = x(600 - 100x + 4x^2)$
  $V = 4x^3 - 100x^2 + 600x$

2 marks total

<note>No marks are awarded for the expansion, but it should be shown for completeness</note>

steps.title: Visualize the cutting process
steps.content:
To model the box geometrically, imagine the flat rectangular sheet. We start with the original dimensions and cut a square of side length $x$ from every corner.

Since we remove a corner from **both** ends of each side, the length and width will both decrease by $2x$.

highlights.text: cutting squares of side length x

steps.title: Deduce the new dimensions
steps.content:
Let's determine the dimensions of the folded box:

*   **Length:** The original length is $30 \text{ cm}$. Cutting $x$ from both the left and right sides leaves us with $(30 - 2x)$.
*   **Width:** The original width is $20 \text{ cm}$. Cutting $x$ from both the top and bottom sides leaves us with $(20 - 2x)$.
*   **Height:** When the sides are folded up, the height of the box equals the side length of the cut squares, which is $x$.

steps.title: Formulate the volume
steps.content:
The volume of a rectangular box is given by the product of its dimensions: $$V = \text{length} \times \text{width} \times \text{height}$$

Substituting our expressions for the dimensions:
$$V = (30 - 2x)(20 - 2x)(x)$$

steps.title: Expand the expression
steps.content:
While the factored form is correct, expanding it is often useful for differentiation in later steps. 

First, expand the brackets for length and width:
$$(30 - 2x)(20 - 2x) = 600 - 60x - 40x + 4x^2 = 4x^2 - 100x + 600$$

Now, multiply by the height $x$:
$$V = x(4x^2 - 100x + 600)$$
$$V = 4x^3 - 100x^2 + 600x$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value of both the factored and expanded forms for a test value like x=2 to verify they are equivalent.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the value of both the factored and expanded forms for a test value like x=2 to verify they are equivalent.

desmos.instructions: Define the volume function V(x) and check the value at x=2.

calculator.description: Verify the algebraic expansion (substituting x=2 as a test case)

parts.content: Determine the value of $x$ that maximizes the volume of the box.
parts.markscheme:
- Express the volume of the box in terms of $x$:
  $V = x(20-2x)(30-2x)$
  $V = x(600 - 100x + 4x^2)$
  $V = 600x - 100x^2 + 4x^3$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Find the derivative of $V$ with respect to $x$:
  $\frac{dV}{dx} = 600 - 200x + 12x^2$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Set the derivative equal to zero and solve for $x$:
  $12x^2 - 200x + 600 = 0$
  
  Using the quadratic formula:
  $x = \frac{200 \pm \sqrt{40000 - 28800}}{24}$
  $x = \frac{200 \pm \sqrt{11200}}{24}$
  $x = \frac{100 \pm 10\sqrt{28}}{12}$
  
  Taking the smaller root (as $x$ must be between 0 and 10):
  $x = \frac{100 - 10\sqrt{28}}{12} \approx 3.92$ cm <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Formulate the volume function
steps.content:
First, we need to express the volume of the box in terms of $x$. The box is made by cutting squares of side $x$ from the corners of a $20 \text{ cm}$ by $30 \text{ cm}$ sheet.

The dimensions of the folded box will be:
- Height $= x$
- Width $= 20 - 2x$
- Length $= 30 - 2x$

The volume $V$ is the product of these dimensions:

$$V(x) = x(20-2x)(30-2x)$$

Expanding this expression to prepare for differentiation:

$$\begin{aligned} V(x) &= x(600 - 40x - 60x + 4x^2) \\ V(x) &= x(600 - 100x + 4x^2) \\ V(x) &= 4x^3 - 100x^2 + 600x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the derivative
steps.content:
To maximize the volume, we use calculus. We find the derivative of $V$ with respect to $x$, denoted as $\frac{dV}{dx}$ or $V'(x)$.

Using the power rule from the data booklet ($f(x)=x^{n} \Rightarrow f'(x)=nx^{n-1}$):

$$\begin{aligned} V(x) &= 4x^3 - 100x^2 + 600x \\ \frac{dV}{dx} &= 3 \cdot 4x^{3-1} - 2 \cdot 100x^{2-1} + 1 \cdot 600x^{1-1} \\ \frac{dV}{dx} &= 12x^2 - 200x + 600 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Set derivative to zero
steps.content:
Maximums occur where the slope of the tangent is zero (stationary points). We set the derivative equal to zero and identify the coefficients for the quadratic formula:

$$12x^2 - 200x + 600 = 0$$

Here, $a = 12$, $b = -200$, and $c = 600$.

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
We use the quadratic formula from the data booklet to find the values of $x$.

$$x = \frac{-(-200) \pm \sqrt{(-200)^2 - 4(12)(600)}}{2(12)}$$

Simplify the terms:
$$\begin{aligned} x &= \frac{200 \pm \sqrt{40000 - 28800}}{24} \\ x &= \frac{200 \pm \sqrt{11200}}{24} \end{aligned}$$

This gives two possible solutions:
$$x_1 \approx 12.74 \quad \text{and} \quad x_2 \approx 3.92$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression for the second root:

desmos.instructions: Calculate the second root:

steps.title: Select the valid solution
steps.content:
We must check which value of $x$ is physically possible. The width of the cardboard is $20 \text{ cm}$, so the cuts must satisfy:
$$20 - 2x > 0 \implies 2x < 20 \implies x < 10$$

Comparing our roots:
- $x \approx 12.74$ is rejected because $12.74 > 10$.
- $x \approx 3.92$ is valid because $0 < 3.92 < 10$.

Therefore, the value that maximizes the volume is approximately $3.92 \text{ cm}$.

steps.title: Verify with GDC
steps.content: You can verify this result by graphing the original volume function $V(x) = x(20-2x)(30-2x)$ and finding the maximum point within the domain $0 < x < 10$.

steps.title: Formulate the volume function
steps.content:
First, we express the volume of the box in terms of $x$. The box is formed by cutting squares of side $x$ from the corners of a $20 \text{ cm}$ by $30 \text{ cm}$ sheet.

The dimensions of the resulting box will be:
- Height: $x$
- Width: $20 - 2x$
- Length: $30 - 2x$

The volume $V$ is the product of these dimensions:
$$V(x) = x(20 - 2x)(30 - 2x)$$

highlights.text: cutting squares of side length x

steps.title: Determine the valid domain
steps.content:
Before graphing, we identify the physical constraints on $x$ to determine the appropriate viewing window. The side lengths must be positive:

1.  $x > 0$
2.  $20 - 2x > 0 \implies 2x < 20 \implies x < 10$
3.  $30 - 2x > 0 \implies 2x < 30 \implies x < 15$

To satisfy all conditions, $x$ must be in the interval:
$$0 < x < 10$$

This helps us set the X-window on our graphing calculator.

steps.title: Find the maximum
steps.content: Use the calculator's maximum finder tool to locate the peak of the graph within the domain $0 < x < 10$. We are looking for the x-coordinate of this peak.

4. Read the x-coordinate

2. A gray dot will appear; hover over or click it to see coordinates

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator identifies the maximum at coordinates approximately $(3.92, 1056)$.

The question asks for the value of $x$ that maximizes the volume. Rounding to 3 significant figures:

$$x \approx 3.92 \text{ cm}$$

parts.content: Verify that the value of $x$ found in part 2 is a maximum by using the second derivative test.
parts.markscheme:
- Find the second derivative of $V$: $\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} = 24x - 200$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute $x = \frac{100 - 10\sqrt{28}}{12}$ into the second derivative:
  $\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} = 24\left(\frac{100 - 10\sqrt{28}}{12}\right) - 200$

- Simplify:
  $\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} = 200 - 20\sqrt{28} - 200 = -20\sqrt{28}$

- Conclude that since $-20\sqrt{28} < 0$, the second derivative is negative, indicating that the critical point is a maximum. <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Find the second derivative
steps.content:
To apply the second derivative test, we first need to find the second derivative of the volume function, $V(x)$. From the previous part, we know the first derivative is:

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = 12x^2 - 200x + 600$$

Differentiating this with respect to $x$ gives the second derivative:

$$\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} = 24x - 200$$

steps.title: Substitute the critical value
steps.content:
Next, we substitute the critical value found in Part 2 into the second derivative. Using the value provided in the markscheme:

$$x = \frac{100 - 10\sqrt{28}}{12}$$

Substituting this for $x$:

$$\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} = 24\left(\frac{100 - 10\sqrt{28}}{12}\right) - 200$$

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.content:
We can simplify the expression by noting that $24 \div 12 = 2$. This allows us to expand the brackets easily:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d^2V}{dx^2} &= 2(100 - 10\sqrt{28}) - 200 \\ &= 200 - 20\sqrt{28} - 200 \\ &= -20\sqrt{28} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In Run-Matrix mode, type:

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen:

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a command line:

calculator.description: Calculate the numerical value of the second derivative to verify it is negative.

steps.title: Conclude the result
steps.content:
The second derivative test states that if $\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} < 0$ at a critical point, that point corresponds to a local maximum.

Since $\sqrt{28}$ is positive, the value $-20\sqrt{28}$ is clearly less than zero.

$$-20\sqrt{28} < 0$$

Therefore, the critical value $x$ is a **maximum**.

parts.content: Calculate the maximum volume of the box.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute $x \approx 3.92$ into the volume formula:
  $V = 4(3.92)^3 - 100(3.92)^2 + 600(3.92)$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the maximum volume:
  $V \approx 1056$ cm³ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<examTip>Remember to include units in your final answer</examTip>

steps.title: Define the volume function
steps.content:
First, we need an expression for the volume of the box. The box is made from a $20 \text{ cm}$ by $30 \text{ cm}$ sheet with squares of side $x$ cut from the corners.

The dimensions of the folded box will be:
- Height: $x$
- Width: $20 - 2x$
- Length: $30 - 2x$

The volume $V$ is the product of these dimensions:

$$V(x) = x(20 - 2x)(30 - 2x)$$

Expanding this expression makes it easier to differentiate:

$$\begin{aligned} V(x) &= x(600 - 40x - 60x + 4x^2) \\ V(x) &= x(600 - 100x + 4x^2) \\ V(x) &= 4x^3 - 100x^2 + 600x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the derivative
steps.content:
To find the maximum volume, we use calculus optimization. We find the derivative of $V$ with respect to $x$ and set it to zero.

Using the power rule from the data booklet ($f(x)=x^{n} \Rightarrow f'(x)=nx^{n-1}$):

$$\begin{aligned} V(x) &= 4x^3 - 100x^2 + 600x \\ \frac{dV}{dx} &= 3(4x^2) - 2(100x) + 1(600) \\ \frac{dV}{dx} &= 12x^2 - 200x + 600 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for the critical value
steps.content:
Set the derivative equal to zero to find the critical values:
$$12x^2 - 200x + 600 = 0$$

Dividing by 4 simplifies the equation:
$$3x^2 - 50x + 150 = 0$$

We can solve this using the quadratic formula from the data booklet:
$$x = \frac{-(-50) \pm \sqrt{(-50)^2 - 4(3)(150)}}{2(3)}$$

$$\begin{aligned} x &= \frac{50 \pm \sqrt{2500 - 1800}}{6} \\ x &= \frac{50 \pm \sqrt{700}}{6} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Go to Equation mode -> Polynomial -> Degree 2. Enter coefficients 3, -50, 150. Solve.

ti84.instructions: Press APPS, select PLYSMLT2, then ROOT FINDER. Set Order to 2. Enter coefficients a=3, b=-50, c=150. Press SOLVE.

desmos.instructions: Type 3x^2 - 50x + 150 = 0 to see the vertical lines at the roots, or type the quadratic formula.

calculator.description: Solve for the roots of the quadratic equation

steps.title: Select the valid x value
steps.content:
The two solutions are:
$$x_1 \approx 3.92 \quad \text{and} \quad x_2 \approx 12.74$$

We must reject $x \approx 12.74$ because the width of the cardboard is only $20 \text{ cm}$. If we cut out two corners of $12.74 \text{ cm}$, the width would be $20 - 2(12.74) = -5.48 \text{ cm}$, which is impossible.

Therefore, the critical value is:
$$x \approx 3.92 \text{ cm}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the fraction values.

ti84.instructions: Enter (50 - sqrt(700)) / 6 and (50 + sqrt(700)) / 6 on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type (50 - sqrt(700)) / 6

calculator.description: Calculate the numerical values of the roots

steps.title: Calculate the maximum volume
steps.content:
Finally, substitute $x \approx 3.92$ back into the original volume formula to find the maximum volume.

$$V = 4(3.92)^3 - 100(3.92)^2 + 600(3.92)$$

Using a calculator for precision:
$$V \approx 1056 \text{ cm}^3$$

gdc.instructions: Substitute the answer from the previous step into the volume equation.

ti84.instructions: Store the exact value of x as X (using STO>). Then type 4X^3 - 100X^2 + 600X.

desmos.instructions: Define f(x) = 4x^3 - 100x^2 + 600x and evaluate f(3.9237...)

calculator.description: Evaluate the volume function at x = 3.9237...

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The maximum volume of the box is approximately $1056 \text{ cm}^3$.

This question uses optimization techniques covered in **Topic 5: Calculus**. If you're unsure about derivatives, review Chapter 5.8.

steps.title: Define the volume function
steps.content:
First, we determine the expression for the volume of the box. Since squares of side length $x$ are cut from each corner of the $20 \text{ cm}$ by $30 \text{ cm}$ cardboard, the dimensions of the folded box will be:

- Height $= x$
- Width $= 20 - 2x$
- Length $= 30 - 2x$

The volume $V$ is the product of these dimensions:

$$V(x) = x(20 - 2x)(30 - 2x)$$

steps.title: Determine the domain
steps.content:
Before graphing, identify the valid domain for $x$ to set an appropriate viewing window. The side lengths must be positive:

$$\begin{aligned} 20 - 2x &> 0 \\ 2x &< 20 \\ x &< 10 \end{aligned}$$

Since $x$ must also be positive, the domain is $0 < x < 10$.

steps.content:
The calculator identifies the maximum point at coordinates approximately $(3.92, 1056)$.

- The $x$-coordinate represents the side length of the cut square: $x \approx 3.92 \text{ cm}$.
- The $y$-coordinate represents the maximum volume: $V \approx 1056 \text{ cm}^3$.

The question asks for the maximum volume, so we take the $y$-value.

questions.specification:
A particle $Q$ moves in a straight path such that after time $t$ seconds, its velocity, $v$ in $\text{m}\,\text{s}^{-1}$, is given by $v = e^{-3t}\sin(6t)$, where $0 < t < \frac{\pi}{2}$.

At time $t$, $Q$ has displacement $s(t)$; at time $t=0$, $s(0) = 0$.

At successive times when the acceleration of $Q$ is $0\,\text{m}\,\text{s}^{-2}$, the velocities of $Q$ form a geometric sequence. The acceleration of $Q$ is zero at times $t_1,$ $t_2,$ $t_3,$ where $t_1 < t_2 < t_3$ and the respective velocities are $v_1,$ $v_2,$ $v_3$.

parts.content: Find the times when $Q$ comes to instantaneous rest.
parts.markscheme:
• $Q$ comes to instantaneous rest when $v = 0$
  • $e^{-3t}\sin(6t) = 0$
  • This occurs when $\sin(6t) = 0$ (since $e^{-3t} \neq 0$ for finite $t$)
  • $6t = n\pi$, where $n$ is an integer
  • $t = \frac{n\pi}{6}$
  • Given $0 < t < \frac{\pi}{2}$, the possible values for $n$ are 1 and 2
  • For $n = 1$: $t = \frac{\pi}{6} \approx 0.524$ A1
  • For $n = 2$: $t = \frac{\pi}{3} \approx 1.05$ A1
2 marks total

steps.title: Identify condition for rest
steps.content:
To find when the particle comes to instantaneous rest, we must set the velocity equal to zero.

$$v = 0$$

steps.title: Apply Null Factor Law
steps.content:
Substitute the given velocity function into the equation:

$$e^{-3t}\sin(6t) = 0$$

According to the Null Factor Law, for a product to be zero, at least one of the factors must be zero.

steps.title: Analyze the exponential term
steps.content:
Consider the first factor, $e^{-3t}$. The exponential function $e^x$ is never equal to zero for any real value of $x$. Therefore:

$$e^{-3t} \neq 0$$

steps.title: Solve the trigonometric equation
steps.content:
Since the exponential term cannot be zero, the sine term must be zero:

$$\sin(6t) = 0$$

The general solution for $\sin(\theta) = 0$ is $\theta = n\pi$, where $n$ is an integer. Applying this to our argument $6t$:

$$\begin{aligned} 6t &= n\pi \\ t &= \frac{n\pi}{6} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Select values within domain
steps.content:
We need to find values of $t$ that satisfy the given domain restriction:

$$0 < t < \frac{\pi}{2}$$

Let's test integer values for $n$:
- If $n=1$: $$t = \frac{1\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{6}$$ (Valid, since $0 < \frac{\pi}{6} < \frac{\pi}{2}$)
- If $n=2$: $$t = \frac{2\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{3}$$ (Valid, since $0 < \frac{\pi}{3} < \frac{\pi}{2}$)
- If $n=3$: $$t = \frac{3\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{2}$$ (Invalid, as the domain is $t < \frac{\pi}{2}$)

gdc.instructions: Calculate pi/6 and pi/3

ti84.instructions: Calculate pi/6 and pi/3 to ensure they are less than pi/2 (approx 1.57)

desmos.instructions: Type pi/6 and pi/3

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The times when the particle comes to instantaneous rest are:

$$t = \frac{\pi}{6}, \quad t = \frac{\pi}{3}$$

steps.title: Identify the condition for rest
steps.content:
The question asks for the times when particle $Q$ comes to **instantaneous rest**. In kinematics, a particle is at rest when its velocity is zero.

So, we must solve the equation:
$$v(t) = 0$$
$$e^{-3t}\sin(6t) = 0$$

steps.title: Plot the function
steps.content:
We will solve this equation graphically by plotting the velocity function $v = e^{-3t}\sin(6t)$ on our GDC. We need to find the $t$-intercepts (roots) where the graph crosses the horizontal axis ($v=0$).

Ensure your calculator is in **Radian** mode and set the window according to the domain given in the question: $0 < t < \frac{\pi}{2}$.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Graph mode.
2. Enter the function: \y1 = e^(-3x) \times \sin(6x)\
3. Set the View Window (V-Window) to Xmin: 0, Xmax: 1.6 (approx π/2).
4. Draw the graph.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MODE] and ensure Radian is selected.
2. Press [Y=] and enter \Y1 = e^(-3X)*sin(6X)\
3. Press [WINDOW] and set:
   Xmin = 0
   Xmax = 1.6 (slightly more than π/2)
   Ymin = -1
   Ymax = 1
4. Press [GRAPH].

desmos.instructions:
1. Type the function: \v(t) = e^{-3t}\sin(6t)\
2. Open Graph Settings (wrench icon) and set the X-axis from -0.1 to 1.7.
3. Make sure the calculator is in Radians (check the bottom of the settings menu).

calculator.description: Plotting the velocity function to find roots

steps.title: Find the intercepts
steps.content:
Use your calculator's root-finding tool to identify the $t$-values where the curve crosses the horizontal axis within the domain $0 < t < 1.57$ (approx $\frac{\pi}{2}$).

The graph shows two intersections in this interval.

2. Select [ROOT].

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Rounding the values from the calculator to 3 significant figures:

First time: $t \approx 0.524$
Second time: $t \approx 1.05$

These correspond to the exact values $t = \frac{\pi}{6}$ and $t = \frac{\pi}{3}$.

parts.content: Find the total distance travelled by $Q$ in the first $1.5$ seconds of its motion.
parts.markscheme:
Method #1

  • Distance required = $\int_0^{1.5} | e^{-3t} \sin(6t) | \, dt$ M1

OR

  • Distance required = $\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{6}} e^{-3t} \sin(6t) \, dt + | \int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} e^{-3t} \sin(6t) \, dt | + \int_{\frac{\pi}{3}}^{1.5} e^{-3t} \sin(6t) \, dt$ M1
  • $= 0.16105\dots + 0.033479\dots + 0.006806\dots$
  • $= 0.201\,\text{m}$ A1

Method #2

  • Using successive minimum and maximum values on the displacement graph M1
  • $0.16105\dots + (0.16105\dots - 0.12757\dots) + (0.13438\dots - 0.12757\dots)$
  • $= 0.201\,\text{m}$ A1
2 marks total

steps.title: Recall distance formula
steps.content:
To find the **total distance travelled** (not just displacement), we must integrate the **absolute value** of the velocity function. This ensures that backward motion counts as positive distance.

$$\text{Distance} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} |v(t)| \, dt$$

steps.title: Set up the integral
steps.content:
Substitute the given velocity function $v = e^{-3t}\sin(6t)$ and the time limits $t=0$ to $t=1.5$ into the distance formula:

$$\text{Distance} = \int_{0}^{1.5} \left| e^{-3t}\sin(6t) \right| \, dt$$

steps.title: Evaluate using GDC
steps.content:
Use the numerical integration feature on your graphing calculator to evaluate the integral directly. 

Ensure your calculator is in **Radian** mode before calculating.

gdc.instructions:
1. Select the definite integral template from the math menu
2. Enter limits 0 and 1.5
3. Select the absolute value function (|x|)
4. Type e^(-3x) × sin(6x) inside the absolute value bars
5. Press EXE/ENTER

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MATH] > 9:fnInt(
2. Set limits from 0 to 1.5
3. Inside the integral, press [MATH] > NUM > 1:abs(
4. Enter the function: e^(-3X) * sin(6X)
5. Enter X as the variable
6. Press [ENTER]

desmos.instructions: Type 'int' to create an integral, then enter the limits 0 and 1.5. Inside, type 'abs' and the function.

calculator.description: Evaluate the definite integral of the absolute value of velocity

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator yields approximately $0.201335\dots$

Rounding to 3 significant figures:

$$\text{Distance} = 0.201 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Strategy: Summing Areas
steps.content:
To find the total distance travelled, we need to integrate the absolute value of the velocity function $|v(t)|$. The **Summing Areas of Intervals** approach involves:

1. Finding the times when velocity is zero ($v=0$) to identify where the particle changes direction.
2. Splitting the motion into separate time intervals based on these zeros.
3. Calculating the area (distance) for each interval individually.
4. Summing the absolute values of these areas to find the total distance.

steps.title: Find velocity zeros
steps.content:
Set velocity to zero to find when the particle changes direction:

$$e^{-3t}\sin(6t) = 0$$

Since $e^{-3t} > 0$ for all $t$, we solve for $\sin(6t) = 0$:

$$6t = n\pi, \quad n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

$$t = \frac{n\pi}{6}$$

We need values within the domain $0 \le t \le 1.5$. Testing values of $n$:
- $n=0 \Rightarrow t = 0$
- $n=1 \Rightarrow t = \frac{\pi}{6} \approx 0.524$
- $n=2 \Rightarrow t = \frac{2\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{3} \approx 1.047$
- $n=3 \Rightarrow t = \frac{3\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{2} \approx 1.571$ (outside $t \le 1.5$)

The velocity is zero at $t = \frac{\pi}{6}$ and $t = \frac{\pi}{3}$ within our range.

gdc.instructions: Enter pi/6 and pi/3 to check their decimal values.

ti84.instructions: Enter pi/6 and pi/3 to check their decimal values.

desmos.instructions: Type pi/6 and pi/3.

calculator.description: Verify the roots are within the range 0 to 1.5

steps.title: Set up integrals
steps.content:
We split the total time $0$ to $1.5$ into three intervals using the zeros found: $[0, \frac{\pi}{6}]$, $[\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{\pi}{3}]$, and $[\frac{\pi}{3}, 1.5]$.

The total distance is the sum of the absolute integrals over these intervals:

$$ \text{Distance} = \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{6}} |v(t)| \, dt + \int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} |v(t)| \, dt + \int_{\frac{\pi}{3}}^{1.5} |v(t)| \, dt $$

Since we are summing areas, we can calculate the definite integral for each segment and take the positive value.

steps.title: Calculate areas
steps.content:
Using your GDC, calculate the integral for each interval:

1.  **Interval 1** ($0$ to $\frac{\pi}{6}$):
    $$ \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{6}} e^{-3t}\sin(6t) \, dt \approx 0.16105 $$

2.  **Interval 2** ($\frac{\pi}{6}$ to $\frac{\pi}{3}$):
    $$ \int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} e^{-3t}\sin(6t) \, dt \approx -0.03348 $$
    Taking the absolute value (distance): $0.03348$

3.  **Interval 3** ($\frac{\pi}{3}$ to $1.5$):
    $$ \int_{\frac{\pi}{3}}^{1.5} e^{-3t}\sin(6t) \, dt \approx 0.00681 $$

*Note: The signs alternate because $\sin(6t)$ oscillates between positive and negative values in these intervals.*

gdc.instructions:
Go to Run-Matrix or Calculation mode.
Select Optn > Calc > Integral (dx).
Enter the function and limits for each of the three intervals.

ti84.instructions:
Press MATH > 9 (fnInt).
Enter the function: e^(-3X)*sin(6X)
Calculate for limits 0 to pi/6, pi/6 to pi/3, and pi/3 to 1.5 separately.

desmos.instructions: Type int(0, pi/6, e^(-3x)sin(6x) dx). Repeat for other intervals.

calculator.description: Calculate the definite integral for each interval

steps.title: Sum total distance
steps.content:
Add the absolute values of the areas from each interval:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Total Distance} &= 0.16105\dots + 0.033479\dots + 0.006806\dots \\ &= 0.20133\dots \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, the total distance is:

$$ 0.201 \text{ m} $$

gdc.instructions: Add the absolute values of the previous results.

ti84.instructions: Add the absolute values of the previous results.

desmos.instructions: Add the values.

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content:
We need to find the total distance travelled by the particle $Q$ in the first $1.5$ seconds. The problem specifies using the **Displacement Turning Points** approach.

This method requires us to:
1. Find the times $t$ where the velocity is zero (turning points).
2. Calculate the displacement $s(t)$ at each turning point and at the endpoints ($t=0$ and $t=1.5$).
3. Sum the absolute differences between these successive displacements.

steps.title: Find turning points
steps.content:
Set the velocity function to zero to find the turning points.

$$v = e^{-3t}\sin(6t) = 0$$

Since $e^{-3t} \neq 0$ for any real $t$, we solve for $\sin(6t) = 0$:

$$6t = k\pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{k\pi}{6}$$

where $k$ is an integer. We need values within the interval $0 < t < 1.5$.

*   When $k=1$: $t = \frac{\pi}{6} \approx 0.524$
*   When $k=2$: $t = \frac{2\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{3} \approx 1.047$
*   When $k=3$: $t = \frac{3\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{2} \approx 1.571$ (This is greater than $1.5$, so we stop here)

Our key time points are $t=0$, $t=\frac{\pi}{6}$, $t=\frac{\pi}{3}$, and the endpoint $t=1.5$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the values of pi/6, pi/3, and pi/2

ti84.instructions: Enter pi/6, pi/3, and pi/2 to see decimal values

desmos.instructions: Type pi/6, pi/3, and pi/2

calculator.description: Calculate pi/6, pi/3, and pi/2 to check which are within the interval 0 to 1.5

steps.title: Calculate displacements
steps.content:
Since $s(0)=0$, the displacement at any time $t$ is the definite integral of velocity from $0$ to $t$:

$$s(t) = \int_0^t v(u) \, du = \int_0^t e^{-3u}\sin(6u) \, du$$

Use your calculator to evaluate this integral at our key time points:

1.  **At $t_1 = \frac{\pi}{6}$:**
    $$s\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \approx 0.16105$$

2.  **At $t_2 = \frac{\pi}{3}$:**
    $$s\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \approx 0.12757$$

3.  **At $t_{\text{end}} = 1.5$:**
    $$s(1.5) \approx 0.13438$$

gdc.instructions: In Run-Matrix or Graph mode, evaluate the definite integral of e^(-3x)*sin(6x) from 0 to pi/6, pi/3, and 1.5.

ti84.instructions: Press MATH -> 9 (fnInt). Enter fnInt(e^(-3X)*sin(6X), X, 0, pi/6). Repeat for limits 0 to pi/3 and 0 to 1.5.

desmos.instructions: Type int(0, pi/6, e^{-3x}sin(6x)dx)

calculator.description: Calculate the definite integrals for displacement at each time point

steps.title: Sum absolute differences
steps.content:
The total distance is the sum of the absolute differences between successive displacements.

$$ \text{Distance} = |s(t_1) - s(0)| + |s(t_2) - s(t_1)| + |s(1.5) - s(t_2)| $$

Substitute the values we found:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Distance} &= |0.16105 - 0| \\ &+ |0.12757 - 0.16105| \\ &+ |0.13438 - 0.12757| \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Distance} &= 0.16105 + |-0.03348| + |0.00681| \\ &= 0.16105 + 0.03348 + 0.00681 \\ &= 0.20134\dots \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to 3 significant figures, the total distance travelled is:

$$0.201 \text{ m}$$

parts.content: Find an expression for $s$ in terms of $t$.
parts.markscheme:
• $s = \int v \, dt = \int e^{-3t} \sin(6t) \, dt$ M1
  • Using integration by parts: $u = \sin(6t)$, $du = 6\cos(6t)\,dt$ $dv = e^{-3t}\,dt$, $v = -\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}$
  • $s = -\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}\sin(6t) - \int (-\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}) \cdot 6\cos(6t)\,dt$ M1
  • $s = -\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}\sin(6t) + 2\int e^{-3t}\cos(6t)\,dt$ A1
  • Integrating the second part again by parts: $u = \cos(6t)$, $du = -6\sin(6t)\,dt$ $dv = e^{-3t}\,dt$, $v = -\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}$
  • $s = -\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}\sin(6t) + 2[-\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}\cos(6t) - 2\int e^{-3t}\sin(6t)\,dt]$ M1
  • $s = -\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}\sin(6t) - \frac{2}{3}e^{-3t}\cos(6t) - 4s$ A1
  • $5s = -\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}\sin(6t) - \frac{2}{3}e^{-3t}\cos(6t)$
  • $s = -\frac{1}{15}e^{-3t}\sin(6t) - \frac{2}{15}e^{-3t}\cos(6t) + C$ A1
  • Since $s(0) = 0$: $0 = -\frac{1}{15}(0) - \frac{2}{15}(1) + C \Rightarrow C = \frac{2}{15}$ M1
  • $s = \frac{2}{15} - \frac{1}{15}e^{-3t}[\sin(6t) + 2\cos(6t)]$ A1
7 marks total

steps.title: Set up the integral
steps.content:
To find the displacement $s$ from the velocity $v$, we need to integrate the velocity function with respect to time. The question states that the displacement starts at $0$ when $t=0$.

$$s(t) = \int v \, dt = \int e^{-3t}\sin(6t) \, dt$$

This integral involves a product of an exponential and a trigonometric function, which is a classic case for **Integration by Parts** (Syllabus Topic AHL 5.16).

steps.title: First integration by parts
steps.content:
We use the integration by parts formula $\int u \frac{dv}{dt} dt = uv - \int v \frac{du}{dt} dt$.

Let's choose:
$$\begin{aligned} u &= \sin(6t) & dv &= e^{-3t} \, dt \\ \frac{du}{dt} &= 6\cos(6t) & v &= -\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t} \end{aligned}$$

Applying the formula:
$$ \int e^{-3t}\sin(6t) \, dt = -\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}\sin(6t) - \int \left(-\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}\right) (6\cos(6t)) \, dt $$

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.content:
Simplifying the constants in the integral term:

$$ \int e^{-3t}\sin(6t) \, dt = -\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}\sin(6t) + 2\int e^{-3t}\cos(6t) \, dt $$

We now have a new integral, $\int e^{-3t}\cos(6t) \, dt$, which requires a second application of integration by parts.

steps.title: Second integration by parts
steps.content:
For the remaining integral $\int e^{-3t}\cos(6t) \, dt$, we apply the method again with the same pattern:

$$\begin{aligned} u &= \cos(6t) & dv &= e^{-3t} \, dt \\ \frac{du}{dt} &= -6\sin(6t) & v &= -\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t} \end{aligned}$$

Applying the formula:
$$ \int e^{-3t}\cos(6t) \, dt = -\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}\cos(6t) - \int \left(-\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}\right)(-6\sin(6t)) \, dt $$

Simplifying this part:
$$ \int e^{-3t}\cos(6t) \, dt = -\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}\cos(6t) - 2\int e^{-3t}\sin(6t) \, dt $$

steps.title: Solve the cyclic equation
steps.content:
Now we substitute this result back into our equation from Step 2. Let $I = \int e^{-3t}\sin(6t) \, dt$ be the integral we are solving for.

$$ I = -\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}\sin(6t) + 2\left[ -\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}\cos(6t) - 2I \right] $$

Expanding the brackets:
$$ I = -\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}\sin(6t) - \frac{2}{3}e^{-3t}\cos(6t) - 4I $$

Add $4I$ to both sides to group the integrals:
$$ 5I = -\frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}\sin(6t) - \frac{2}{3}e^{-3t}\cos(6t) $$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 2 * -2

ti84.instructions: Calculate 2 * -2

desmos.instructions: Calculate 2 * -2

calculator.description: Calculate the coefficient of I on the RHS

steps.title: Isolate the displacement function
steps.content:
Divide by 5 to solve for $I$, which gives us the general form for the displacement $s(t)$. Don't forget to add the constant of integration $C$.

$$ s(t) = -\frac{1}{15}e^{-3t}\sin(6t) - \frac{2}{15}e^{-3t}\cos(6t) + C $$

We can factor out $-\frac{1}{15}e^{-3t}$ to make it neater:
$$ s(t) = -\frac{1}{15}e^{-3t}[\sin(6t) + 2\cos(6t)] + C $$

steps.title: Find constant C
steps.content:
We are given the initial condition $s(0) = 0$. Substitute $t=0$ into the equation to find $C$. Note that $e^0 = 1$, $\sin(0) = 0$, and $\cos(0) = 1$.

$$\begin{aligned} 0 &= -\frac{1}{15}e^{0}[\sin(0) + 2\cos(0)] + C \\ 0 &= -\frac{1}{15}(1)[0 + 2(1)] + C \\ 0 &= -\frac{2}{15} + C \\ C &= \frac{2}{15} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Substitute $C = \frac{2}{15}$ back into the expression for $s(t)$.

$$ s = \frac{2}{15} - \frac{1}{15}e^{-3t}[\sin(6t) + 2\cos(6t)] $$

parts.content: Show that, at these times, $\tan 6t = 2$.
parts.markscheme:
• Acceleration $a = \frac{dv}{dt}$
  • $a = -3e^{-3t}\sin(6t) + 6e^{-3t}\cos(6t)$ M1
  • At these times, $a = 0$:
  • $3e^{-3t}(2\cos(6t) - \sin(6t)) = 0$
  • Since $e^{-3t} \neq 0$, $2\cos(6t) - \sin(6t) = 0$
  • $\sin(6t) = 2\cos(6t)$
  • $\tan(6t) = 2$ A1AG
2 marks total

steps.title: Recall relationship between kinematic variables
steps.content:
To find the times when the acceleration is zero, we first need to find an expression for acceleration. Acceleration $a(t)$ is the derivative of velocity $v(t)$ with respect to time.

$$a(t) = \frac{dv}{dt}$$

steps.title: Apply the product rule
steps.content:
We need to differentiate the velocity function $v = e^{-3t}\sin(6t)$. Since this is a product of two functions, $e^{-3t}$ and $\sin(6t)$, we use the product rule:

$$\frac{d}{dt}(uv) = u\frac{dv}{dt} + v\frac{du}{dt}$$ 

Let $u = e^{-3t}$ and $v_{part} = \sin(6t)$. Using the chain rule on standard derivatives from the data booklet:

- $\frac{du}{dt} = -3e^{-3t}$
- $\frac{dv_{part}}{dt} = 6\cos(6t)$

steps.title: Find the expression for acceleration
steps.content:
Substituting these derivatives back into the product rule gives the expression for acceleration:

$$\begin{aligned} a(t) &= e^{-3t}(6\cos(6t)) + \sin(6t)(-3e^{-3t}) \\ a(t) &= 6e^{-3t}\cos(6t) - 3e^{-3t}\sin(6t) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Set acceleration to zero
steps.content:
The question asks for the times when the acceleration is zero. We equate our expression to $0$ and factor out the common term $3e^{-3t}$:

$$\begin{aligned} 6e^{-3t}\cos(6t) - 3e^{-3t}\sin(6t) &= 0 \\ 3e^{-3t}(2\cos(6t) - \sin(6t)) &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Isolate the trigonometric terms
steps.content:
Since the exponential function $e^{-3t}$ is never zero ($e^x > 0$ for all real $x$), we can divide both sides by $3e^{-3t}$ without losing solutions. This leaves us with:

$$2\cos(6t) - \sin(6t) = 0$$

steps.title: Rearrange to show the result
steps.content:
Now we rearrange the equation to find $\tan(6t)$. Add $\sin(6t)$ to both sides and then divide by $\cos(6t)$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2\cos(6t) &= \sin(6t) \\ \frac{2\cos(6t)}{\cos(6t)} &= \frac{\sin(6t)}{\cos(6t)} \\ 2 &= \tan(6t) \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, $\tan(6t) = 2$ as required.

steps.title: Rewrite velocity as a quotient
steps.content:
To apply the quotient rule, we first rewrite the given velocity function $v = e^{-3t}\sin(6t)$ as a fraction. By moving the term with the negative exponent to the denominator, we get:

$$v = \frac{\sin(6t)}{e^{3t}}$$

This form $v = \frac{u}{w}$ allows us to use the quotient rule for differentiation, which is part of **Topic 5: Calculus** (Syllabus SL 5.6).

steps.title: Differentiate numerator and denominator
steps.content:
We identify the numerator $u = \sin(6t)$ and the denominator $w = e^{3t}$. Using the chain rule and the standard derivatives from the data booklet:

For the numerator:
$$\frac{du}{dt} = 6\cos(6t)$$

For the denominator:
$$\frac{dw}{dt} = 3e^{3t}$$

steps.title: Apply the quotient rule
steps.content:
Acceleration $a$ is the derivative of velocity $\frac{dv}{dt}$. Applying the quotient rule formula $\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{w\frac{du}{dx} - u\frac{dw}{dx}}{w^2}$:

$$a = \frac{e^{3t}(6\cos(6t)) - \sin(6t)(3e^{3t})}{(e^{3t})^2}$$

$$a = \frac{6e^{3t}\cos(6t) - 3e^{3t}\sin(6t)}{e^{6t}}$$ 

This matches the method required to find the times when the acceleration is zero.

steps.title: Equate numerator to zero
steps.content:
We are looking for the times when $a = 0$. For a fraction to be zero, its numerator must be zero (assuming the denominator is non-zero). 

$$6e^{3t}\cos(6t) - 3e^{3t}\sin(6t) = 0$$

We can factor out the common term $3e^{3t}$:

$$3e^{3t}(2\cos(6t) - \sin(6t)) = 0$$

Since the exponential function $e^{3t}$ is never zero for any real $t$, we can divide it out, leaving:

$$2\cos(6t) - \sin(6t) = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for tan(6t)
steps.content:
Finally, we rearrange the equation to show the required relationship. 

Add $\sin(6t)$ to both sides:
$$2\cos(6t) = \sin(6t)$$

Divide both sides by $\cos(6t)$:
$$2 = \frac{\sin(6t)}{\cos(6t)}$$

Using the identity $\tan \theta = \frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}$, we obtain:
$$\tan(6t) = 2$$

This confirms the relationship required by the question.

2. Find the root (zero) of the function.
3. Store this x-value.
4. Calculate tan(6x) in the calculation view.

parts.content: Show that the velocities $v_1, v_2, v_3$ form a geometric sequence.
parts.markscheme:
• Attempt to evaluate $t_1, t_2, t_3$ in exact form M1
  • $6t_1 = \arctan 2 \Rightarrow t_1 = \frac{1}{6} \arctan 2$
  • $6t_2 = \pi + \arctan 2 \Rightarrow t_2 = \frac{\pi}{6} + \frac{1}{6} \arctan 2$
  • $6t_3 = 2\pi + \arctan 2 \Rightarrow t_3 = \frac{\pi}{3} + \frac{1}{6} \arctan 2$
The A1 is for any two consecutive correct, or showing that $6t_2 = \pi + 6t_1$ or $6t_3 = 2\pi + 6t_2$.
  • Showing that $\sin(6t_{n+1}) = -\sin(6t_n)$ M1
  • e.g., $\tan 6t = 2 \Rightarrow \sin 6t = \pm \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$ A1
  • Showing that $\frac{e^{-3(t_{n+1})}}{e^{-3t_n}} = e^{-\frac{\pi}{2}}$ M1
  • e.g., $e^{-3(\frac{\pi}{6}+k)} \div e^{-3k} = e^{-\frac{\pi}{2}}$
Award the A1 for any two consecutive terms.
  • $\frac{v_3}{v_2} = \frac{v_2}{v_1} = -e^{-\frac{\pi}{2}}$ AG
5 marks total

steps.title: Evaluate acceleration
steps.content:
To find the times when acceleration is zero, we first need to find the acceleration function $a(t)$ by differentiating the velocity $v = e^{-3t}\sin(6t)$. Using the product rule and the standard derivatives:

$$a(t) = \frac{d}{dt}(e^{-3t})\sin(6t) + e^{-3t}\frac{d}{dt}(\sin(6t))$$

$$a(t) = -3e^{-3t}\sin(6t) + 6e^{-3t}\cos(6t)$$

steps.title: Solve for times
steps.content:
We set $a(t) = 0$ to find the times $t_1, t_2, t_3$. Since $e^{-3t}$ is never zero, we can divide by it:

$$-3e^{-3t}\sin(6t) + 6e^{-3t}\cos(6t) = 0$$
$$-3\sin(6t) + 6\cos(6t) = 0$$
$$3\sin(6t) = 6\cos(6t)$$
$$\tan(6t) = 2$$

The general solution is $6t = \arctan(2) + k\pi$, or $t = \frac{1}{6}\arctan(2) + \frac{k\pi}{6}$.
